relationship: This file contains supplementary ERA 1996 sections that extend the primary content file. The KLIA-001 embedding pipeline indexes both files via kl_provisions vector search. Eileen retrieves sections by semantic match regardless of which file they are in. The primary file (era1996.json) contains the 24 crown jewel sections covering the complete unfair dismissal lifecycle, redundancy payments architecture, whistleblowing claim path, and compensation framework.

sections.title: Right to time off for public duties
sections.summary: An employer shall permit an employee who holds specified public offices to take a REASONABLE amount of UNPAID time off during working hours for the purpose of performing the duties of that office. The right applies to: justices of the peace (magistrates); members of local authorities (councillors); members of statutory tribunals; members of NHS bodies (ICBs, NHS trusts, NHS foundation trusts); members of governing bodies of maintained schools, academies, and further education institutions; members of the Environment Agency, Natural Resources Wales, Scottish Environment Protection Agency; prison visitors/prison monitoring boards; and members of water and sewerage undertakers (England/Wales) and Scottish Water. This is a DAY-ONE right — no qualifying period. The time off is UNPAID unless the employer's policy provides otherwise. The right is for EMPLOYEES only (not workers). Jury service is NOT covered by s.50 — separate provisions apply (ss.43M and s.98B). ERA 2025 includes a review of the time off for public duties provisions.
sections.text:
(1) An employer shall permit an employee of his who is a justice of the peace to take time off during the employee's working hours for the purpose of performing any of the duties of his office.

(2) An employer shall permit an employee of his who is a member of—

(a) a local authority,
(b) a statutory tribunal,
(d) a relevant health body,
(e) a relevant education body,
(f) the Environment Agency or the Scottish Environment Protection Agency,
(g) Scottish Water or a Water Industry Commission for Scotland,
(h) a prison visiting committee, a ## board or a prison monitoring committee,
(i) Natural Resources Wales,
(j) an integrated care board established under section 14Z25 of the National Health Service Act 2006,
(k) a board of an NHS foundation trust,

to take time off during the employee's working hours for the purposes specified in subsection (3).

(3) The purposes referred to in subsection (2) are—

(a) attendance at a meeting of the body or any of its committees or sub-committees,
(b) the doing of any other thing approved by the body, or any of its committees or sub-committees, for the purpose of the discharge of the functions of the body or of the committee or sub-committee, and
(c) in the case of a relevant education body, the doing of anything for the purpose of the discharge of the functions of the governing body which is so approved or required under an enactment.

(4) The amount of time off which an employee is to be permitted to take under this section, and the occasions on which and any conditions subject to which time off may be so taken, are those that are reasonable in all the circumstances having regard, in particular, to—

(a) how much time off is required for the performance of the duties of the office or as a member of the body in question, and the amount of time off required for the performance of the duties of any other office held by the employee or for membership of any other body of which the employee is a member,

(b) how much time off the employee has already been permitted under this section or sections 168 or 168A of the Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992, and

(c) the circumstances of the employer's business and the effect of the employee's absence on the running of that business.
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 50 balances the public interest in civic participation with the employer's operational needs. Unlike s.55 (ante-natal care — absolute right, no employer discretion), s.50 requires the employer to permit REASONABLE time off — and reasonableness is assessed having regard to the employer's circumstances. The employer can consider: the employee's other public duties, time already taken for public duties or trade union duties, and the effect of the absence on the business (s.50(4)). This is the ONLY time-off provision in Part VI where the employer's business impact is a relevant factor.
coveredOffices: Member of a local authority (councillor) — s.50(2)(a)
Member of a statutory tribunal — s.50(2)(b)
Member of a relevant health body (ICB, NHS Trust, NHS Foundation Trust) — s.50(2)(d), (j), (k)
Member of a relevant education body (maintained school, academy, FE institution governing body) — s.50(2)(e)
Member of the Environment Agency, Natural Resources Wales, or SEPA — s.50(2)(f), (i)
Member of Scottish Water or Water Industry Commission — s.50(2)(g)
Prison visiting committee, prison monitoring board — s.50(2)(h)

notCovered.juryService: Jury service is NOT covered by s.50. Separate provisions apply: s.43M (right not to suffer detriment for jury service) and s.98B (dismissal for jury service is automatically unfair unless 'substantial injury' to employer's undertaking and employee unreasonably failed to seek deferral). The employer is not required to pay during jury service — the employee claims loss of earnings from the court.
notCovered.specialConstables: Special constables are NOT covered by s.50. An amendment to include them was proposed during the passage of the Employment Rights Bill in January 2025 but was rejected by the Government.
notCovered.otherVolunteering: Voluntary work for charities, hospitals, campaigning, or other organisations is NOT covered by s.50. The employee has no statutory right to time off for these activities unless the employer's policy or contract provides otherwise.

factors: (a) How much time off is required for the duties — and the time off required for any OTHER public duties the employee holds
(b) How much time off the employee has ALREADY been permitted under s.50 or for trade union duties under TULRCA ss.168/168A
(c) The circumstances of the employer's BUSINESS and the effect of the absence on the running of that business
whatIsReasonable.practicalNote: Unlike s.57A (dependants — employer's business impact is NOT relevant), s.50(4)(c) expressly makes the employer's business circumstances a factor. A small employer with limited cover may reasonably permit less time off than a large employer with extensive cover. The employee who holds multiple public offices cannot expect unlimited time off from each.

protections.complaint: s.51 — the employee may present a complaint to the tribunal that the employer unreasonably refused to permit time off under s.50. The tribunal may declare the failure and award compensation (just and equitable).
protections.dismissal: s.104 — dismissal for asserting a statutory right (including the s.50 right) is automatically unfair. No qualifying period.
protections.detriment: s.47C — applies generally to detriment for exercising employment rights, though s.50 is not specifically listed in s.47C. The employee may also have an ordinary unfair dismissal claim if dismissed for taking time off.

versions.text: [s.50(1) was substituted and s.50(2)(i)–(k) were inserted by the Time Off for Public Duties Order 2018 (SI 2018/665). The list of bodies has been amended multiple times since 1996 to reflect changes in public body structures (NHS restructuring, Environment Agency, Natural Resources Wales, etc.).]
versions.amendedBy: ERA 1996 as enacted; updated by SI 2018/665, Health and Care Act 2022 Sch.1 para.1, and various other instruments

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 includes a provision for a REVIEW of the circumstances in which employers are required to permit employees to take time off for public duties. The Government's implementation roadmap does not specify a date for this review but it is expected during 2026/2027. The review may result in changes to the list of covered bodies, the reasonableness test, or the introduction of a right to PAID time off for certain duties.
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: LOW currently — the review has not yet begun. If the review recommends expanding s.50 (e.g., adding special constables, introducing paid time off), the impact could be MODERATE.

leadingCases.name: Borders Regional Council v Maule
leadingCases.principle: The assessment of what is 'reasonable' time off under s.50 must take into account ALL the factors in s.50(4), including the employer's circumstances. A blanket refusal to permit ANY time off is unreasonable. But the employer is entitled to consider the operational impact and to limit the time off if the employee's absence would have a significant effect on the business.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the employer must carry out a genuine assessment of reasonableness, balancing the employee's need to perform public duties against the employer's operational requirements. A blanket refusal is unreasonable; a reasonable limitation after genuine consideration is lawful.
leadingCases.significance: THE foundational authority on the s.50(4) reasonableness test. Establishes that the employer must genuinely consider the request — not simply refuse. But also confirms that the employer's business needs are a legitimate factor.

leadingCases.name: Corner v Buckinghamshire County Council
leadingCases.principle: The 'duties' of a magistrate under s.50(1) include not only sitting in court but also training, administrative duties, and other activities that are part of the magistrate's role. The employer must permit time off for all duties of the office, not just court appearances.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the duties of a justice of the peace include all activities forming part of the role — not only sitting in court. Training sessions, committee meetings, and administrative responsibilities are within scope.
leadingCases.significance: Broad interpretation of 'duties' — covers the full scope of the public office, not just the most visible activities.

leadingCases.name: Emmott v Michael Wilson & Partners Ltd
leadingCases.principle: Dismissal for taking time off for public duties may be both an automatically unfair dismissal under s.104 (asserting a statutory right) and an ordinary unfair dismissal under s.98. The employee can pursue both claims. The s.104 route has no qualifying period.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal confirmed that an employee dismissed for exercising a statutory right (including the s.50 right to time off for public duties) may claim under s.104 (no qualifying period) as well as ordinary unfair dismissal under s.98 (qualifying period applies).
leadingCases.significance: Confirms the dual claim route for employees dismissed for taking public duty time off. The s.104 route provides day-one protection — the employer cannot dismiss a magistrate or councillor for exercising their s.50 rights regardless of length of service.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Time off for public duties
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance on the right to time off for public duties including the list of covered offices.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Time off for public duties
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance on the employer's obligations and the reasonableness assessment.

commonErrors: Confusing s.50 (public duties) with jury service — jury service is NOT covered by s.50. Separate provisions apply: s.43M (detriment for jury service) and s.98B (dismissal for jury service — automatically unfair unless substantial injury to undertaking and employee unreasonably failed to seek deferral). The employee claims loss of earnings from the court, not the employer

Assuming the employer must pay for time off under s.50 — s.50 provides UNPAID time off. The employer has no statutory obligation to pay the employee for time off taken for public duties. Many employers choose to pay (particularly for magistrates) but this is a policy decision, not a legal obligation. The employee may be able to claim loss of earnings allowances from the relevant public body

Applying a blanket refusal to all requests — a blanket refusal to permit ANY time off for public duties is unreasonable (Borders Regional Council v Maule [1993]). The employer must genuinely consider each request, applying the s.50(4) factors. However, the employer can reasonably limit the time off having considered the operational impact

Not considering ALL the s.50(4) factors — when assessing reasonableness, the employer must consider: (a) how much time is needed for THIS public duty AND any OTHER public duties the employee holds; (b) how much s.50/trade union time off the employee has ALREADY taken; (c) the effect on the employer's business. Ignoring any of these factors may render the decision unreasonable

Interpreting 'duties' narrowly — the duties of a public office include all activities forming part of the role, not just the most visible activities. For magistrates: court sittings, training, committee meetings, site visits. For councillors: full council, committee meetings, surgeries, site inspections. For school governors: governing body meetings, training, disciplinary panels, recruitment panels (Corner v Buckinghamshire [1978])

Not recognising that dismissal for exercising s.50 rights is automatically unfair under s.104 — dismissal for asserting the statutory right to time off for public duties is automatically unfair with NO qualifying period. The employer cannot dismiss a magistrate, councillor, or school governor for taking time off that is reasonable under s.50 (Emmott v Michael Wilson [2008] EWCA)

Assuming special constables are covered — special constables are NOT covered by s.50. An amendment to include them was proposed and rejected during the passage of the Employment Rights Bill in January 2025. Special constables have no statutory right to time off for their policing duties

Not having a public duties policy — while not a statutory requirement, a clear policy on public duties helps the employer demonstrate that requests are assessed consistently and reasonably. The policy should address: how requests are made, who considers them, what factors are weighed, whether paid or unpaid, and the employer's expectations on notice and record-keeping

Treating all public duty absences the same regardless of employer size — s.50(4)(c) expressly makes the employer's business circumstances relevant. A sole trader with one employee may reasonably permit less time off than a government department with extensive cover. The assessment is proportionate to the employer's capacity

Not knowing that ERA 2025 includes a review of public duties provisions — the Government has announced a review of the time off for public duties framework as part of the ERA 2025 implementation. This may result in changes to the list of covered bodies, the reasonableness test, or the introduction of paid time off for certain duties. Employers should monitor developments

Not recognising that voluntary work and charity board membership are NOT covered — s.50 applies only to the listed public offices and bodies. Voluntary work for charities, community organisations, or non-listed bodies is not within scope. The employee has no statutory right to time off for these activities — any time off is at the employer's discretion or governed by contract/policy

sections.title: Right to time off for ante-natal care
sections.summary: An employee who is pregnant and who, on the advice of a registered medical practitioner, registered midwife or registered nurse, has made an appointment to receive ante-natal care is entitled to be permitted by her employer to take time off during working hours to keep the appointment. This is a DAY-ONE right — no qualifying period. The time off is PAID at the employee's normal hourly rate (s.56). The employer cannot require evidence for the FIRST appointment; for subsequent appointments, the employer may request a pregnancy certificate and appointment card (s.55(2)). 'Ante-natal care' is not defined in the statute but is interpreted broadly to include scans, midwife appointments, hospital check-ups, parentcraft classes, and relaxation classes — provided they are recommended by a registered medical professional. Partners have a separate right to UNPAID time off to accompany the pregnant employee to up to two ante-natal appointments (s.57ZE, Children and Families Act 2014 — max 6.5 hours per occasion). Dismissal for exercising the right is automatically unfair (s.99).
sections.text:
(1) An employee who—

(a) is pregnant, and

(b) has, on the advice of a registered medical practitioner, registered midwife or registered nurse, made an appointment to attend at any place for the purpose of receiving ante-natal care,

is entitled to be permitted by her employer to take time off during the employee's working hours in order to enable her to keep the appointment.

(2) An employee is not entitled to take time off under this section to keep an appointment unless, if her employer requests her to do so, she produces for his inspection—

(a) a certificate from a registered medical practitioner, registered midwife or registered nurse stating that the employee is pregnant, and

(b) an appointment card or some other document showing that the appointment has been made.

(3) Subsection (2) does not apply where the employee's appointment is the first appointment during her pregnancy for which she seeks permission to take time off in accordance with subsection (1).
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 55 creates an ABSOLUTE RIGHT to time off for ante-natal care. Unlike s.57A (dependants — 'reasonable' amount), s.55 does not limit the amount or duration — the employee is entitled to take whatever time is necessary to attend the appointment, including travel time and waiting time. The right is PAID (s.56 — at the employee's normal hourly rate). The right applies from day one of employment. The employer cannot refuse if the statutory conditions are met.

paidRight.entitlement: An employee who is permitted to take time off under s.55 is entitled to be PAID remuneration by her employer for the period of absence at the appropriate hourly rate. The appropriate hourly rate is the amount of one week's pay divided by the number of normal working hours. Where hours vary, the average over the period is used.
paidRight.includesTravel: The paid time off includes travel time to and from the appointment and any waiting time at the appointment. The employer cannot require the employee to make up the time or take the absence as annual leave.
paidRight.contrast: This is a critical distinction from s.57A (dependants — UNPAID) and s.57ZE (partner's ante-natal — UNPAID). Section 55 is one of the few day-one rights that is PAID.

evidenceRequirements.firstAppointment: s.55(3) — no evidence can be required for the FIRST ante-natal appointment. The employee simply informs the employer of the appointment.
evidenceRequirements.subsequentAppointments: s.55(2) — for subsequent appointments, the employer MAY (not must) request: (a) a certificate from a registered medical practitioner, midwife, or nurse stating that the employee is pregnant; AND (b) an appointment card or other document showing the appointment has been made. The employer cannot demand a MATB1 (not issued until 20 weeks) — a GP or midwife letter is sufficient.
evidenceRequirements.practicalNote: Best practice is for the employer to make a single request for the pregnancy certificate and then accept appointment cards for subsequent appointments. Repeatedly requesting new pregnancy certificates is likely to be seen as harassment.

whatIsAntenatalCare.statutoryDefinition: There is NO statutory definition of 'ante-natal care'. It is interpreted broadly.
includes: Hospital scans (dating scan, anomaly scan, growth scans)
Parentcraft and antenatal classes (if recommended by a medical professional)
Relaxation and breathing classes (if recommended by a medical professional)
Smoking cessation classes (if recommended as part of ante-natal care)
Any appointment made on the advice of a registered medical practitioner, midwife, or nurse
doesNotInclude: Appointments not made on the advice of a registered medical professional (e.g., privately arranged complementary therapies without medical recommendation)
General health appointments unrelated to pregnancy
whatIsAntenatalCare.ivf: For IVF treatment, 'pregnancy' begins at the point of implantation of the fertilised ova — not at the point of egg collection or fertilisation (Mayr v Bäckerei und Konditorei Gerhard Flöckner OHG [2008] IRLR 387, CJEU). The right to time off under s.55 arises from the point of implantation.

partnersRight.provision: s.57ZE ERA 1996 (inserted by Children and Families Act 2014 s.127)
partnersRight.entitlement: An employee who has a 'qualifying relationship' with a pregnant woman or her expected child is entitled to UNPAID time off to accompany the pregnant woman to ante-natal appointments.
partnersRight.limits: Maximum 2 occasions per pregnancy. Maximum 6.5 hours per occasion.
partnersRight.qualifyingRelationship: Spouse, civil partner, partner living in enduring family relationship, father of the expected child (including by virtue of HFEA 2008 ss.42/43), potential applicant for parental order under HFEA 2008 s.54.
partnersRight.unpaid: The partner's right is UNPAID (unlike the pregnant employee's right which is paid). The employer may choose to pay, but has no obligation to do so.
partnersRight.evidenceRequirement: The employer may request a signed declaration from the employee confirming the qualifying relationship and that the appointment is on medical advice.

protections.detriment: s.47C ERA 1996 — an employee has the right not to be subjected to any detriment for exercising the right to time off under s.55.
protections.dismissal: s.99 ERA 1996 — dismissal for a reason connected to pregnancy, childbirth, or the exercise of rights under s.55 is AUTOMATICALLY UNFAIR. No qualifying period. This includes dismissal for taking time off for ante-natal care, for the frequency of appointments, or for any pregnancy-related reason.
protections.complaint: s.57 ERA 1996 — the employee may present a complaint to the tribunal if the employer: (a) unreasonably refused to permit time off; OR (b) failed to pay the employee for the time off. The tribunal may order the employer to pay the remuneration that should have been paid.

versions.text: [s.55 has been substantively unchanged since enactment. Minor amendments: 'registered health visitor' replaced by 'registered nurse' by the Nursing and Midwifery Order 2001 (SI 2002/253). The provision originally derived from EPCA 1978 s.31, which itself implemented the EU Pregnant Workers Directive (92/85/EEC).]
versions.amendedBy: ERA 1996 as enacted; minor textual amendment by SI 2002/253 (nursing/midwifery register); s.57ZE (partner's right) added by Children and Families Act 2014
versions.policyRationale: The right to paid time off for ante-natal care was one of the first pregnancy protections in UK employment law. It ensures that pregnant employees are not financially penalised for attending medical appointments that are essential for their health and the health of the baby.

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 does not directly amend s.55. However, the enhanced dismissal protections for pregnant women (expected 2027) will strengthen the s.99 auto-unfair dismissal protection by potentially restricting the circumstances in which a pregnant employee can be dismissed at all — creating an even stronger protective framework around the exercise of s.55 rights.
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: Enhanced pregnancy dismissal protections: expected 2027 (consultation ongoing).
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: LOW for s.55 directly — the right is unchanged. The enhanced pregnancy dismissal protections under ERA 2025 will create additional exposure for employers who dismiss pregnant employees for any reason.

leadingCases.name: Gregory v Tudsbury Ltd
leadingCases.principle: The right to time off for ante-natal care includes the time spent travelling to and from the appointment, not just the time spent at the appointment itself. The employer must pay for the entire period of absence including travel time.
leadingCases.heldText: The tribunal held that the right to time off under what is now s.55 includes travel time and waiting time. The employer cannot limit the paid time off to only the duration of the medical consultation itself.
leadingCases.significance: Establishes that 'time off to keep the appointment' encompasses the full period from leaving work to returning — including travel and waiting. Important because employers who try to limit payment to the consultation time alone are breaching s.56.

leadingCases.name: Mayr v Bäckerei und Konditorei Gerhard Flöckner OHG
leadingCases.principle: For IVF treatment, 'pregnancy' under the Pregnant Workers Directive (and therefore s.55) begins at the point of IMPLANTATION of the fertilised ova in the uterus — not at the point of egg collection or fertilisation. The right to time off for ante-natal care arises from implantation, not before.
leadingCases.heldText: The CJEU held that a woman undergoing IVF is not 'pregnant' within the meaning of the Directive until the fertilised ova have been transferred to her uterus. Before implantation, the specific pregnancy protections do not apply — though general sex discrimination protections may.
leadingCases.significance: Defines the start point of pregnancy for s.55 purposes in IVF cases. Critical for fertility treatment — the s.55 right does not cover egg collection or fertilisation appointments, only post-implantation ante-natal care.

leadingCases.name: Visa International Service Association v Paul
leadingCases.principle: Dismissal of a pregnant employee shortly after she requested time off for ante-natal appointments gives rise to a strong inference that the pregnancy was the reason for the dismissal. The employer must demonstrate a genuine, non-pregnancy-related reason for the dismissal.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT upheld the finding that the dismissal of the employee during her probationary period, shortly after she disclosed her pregnancy and requested time off for ante-natal appointments, was automatically unfair under s.99.
leadingCases.significance: Demonstrates that the s.99 auto-unfair protection applies with full force during probationary periods. An employer who dismisses a probationary employee after she discloses pregnancy and requests ante-natal time off faces a strong inferential case.

leadingCases.name: Pedersen v Camden London Borough Council
leadingCases.principle: Ante-natal care includes parentcraft and relaxation classes when these are recommended by a registered medical professional as part of the employee's ante-natal care programme. The right is not limited to purely medical appointments.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that ante-natal care should be interpreted broadly to include classes and activities recommended by medical professionals as part of the employee's overall ante-natal care programme.
leadingCases.significance: THE foundational authority on the broad interpretation of 'ante-natal care'. Establishes that the right extends beyond clinical appointments to include any care activity recommended by a registered professional.

leadingCases.name: Batson v Calmac Ferries Ltd
leadingCases.principle: An employer who subjects a pregnant employee to detriment for the frequency of ante-natal appointments (e.g., questioning why she has 'so many' appointments, suggesting she should schedule them outside work hours, or treating the absences as performance issues) is in breach of s.47C.
leadingCases.heldText: The tribunal found that the employer's treatment of the employee — including questioning the frequency of appointments and making comments about the impact on the team — constituted a detriment connected to her pregnancy and exercise of ante-natal care rights.
leadingCases.significance: Demonstrates that the protective framework extends beyond outright refusal — any adverse treatment connected to ante-natal appointment frequency is a detriment. Employers must not pressure employees about the number or timing of appointments.

regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance on the right to paid time off for ante-natal care, including what counts as ante-natal care.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Working when pregnant: your rights
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance on pregnancy rights at work including ante-natal care time off.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Time off for antenatal care
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Detailed guidance including evidence requirements and partner's rights.

commonErrors: Treating the right to ante-natal care time off as unpaid — s.55/s.56 provide a PAID right. The employee is entitled to be paid at her normal hourly rate for the entire period of absence including travel and waiting time (Gregory v Tudsbury [1982]). This is distinct from s.57A (dependants — UNPAID) and s.57ZE (partner's ante-natal — UNPAID)

Requiring evidence for the FIRST ante-natal appointment — s.55(3) expressly provides that the employer CANNOT require a pregnancy certificate or appointment card for the first appointment. Only subsequent appointments can trigger the evidence requirement in s.55(2). Requiring evidence for the first appointment is an unreasonable refusal

Insisting on a MATB1 certificate as proof of pregnancy — the MATB1 is not issued until 20 weeks of pregnancy. The employer must accept a certificate from a GP, midwife, or registered nurse stating that the employee is pregnant (s.55(2)(a)). Insisting on a MATB1 effectively denies the right for the first 20 weeks of pregnancy

Interpreting 'ante-natal care' narrowly — 'ante-natal care' is not defined in the statute and is interpreted BROADLY. It includes scans, midwife appointments, blood tests, parentcraft classes, relaxation classes, and any other appointment made on the advice of a registered medical professional (Pedersen v Camden [1981]). The employer cannot restrict the right to clinical appointments only

Not recognising that the partner also has rights — s.57ZE (CFA 2014) gives partners (spouses, civil partners, qualifying relationships) the right to UNPAID time off to accompany the pregnant employee to up to 2 ante-natal appointments, maximum 6.5 hours each. This is a separate right with different conditions (unpaid, capped)

Pressuring employees about the frequency or timing of appointments — questioning why the employee has 'so many' appointments, suggesting she should schedule them outside work hours, or treating ante-natal absences as performance issues constitutes a detriment under s.47C. Employers must not pressure employees about the number, timing, or duration of appointments

Not knowing that the right applies from DAY ONE — s.55 has no qualifying period. A pregnant employee on her first day of employment has the full right to paid time off for ante-natal care. The employer cannot refuse on the basis of short service or probationary status

Not recognising that dismissal for ante-natal care is automatically unfair — s.99 makes dismissal for a reason connected to pregnancy or the exercise of s.55 rights automatically unfair with NO qualifying period. This applies during probationary periods and regardless of length of service (Visa International v Paul [2004])

Requiring the employee to make up the time or use annual leave — the employer CANNOT require the employee to make up the time taken for ante-natal appointments or to take the absence as annual leave. The right is to paid time off FROM WORK, not an alternative form of absence. Making the employee work additional hours to compensate is a breach

Not understanding when pregnancy begins for IVF — for IVF treatment, pregnancy (and therefore the s.55 right) begins at IMPLANTATION, not at egg collection or fertilisation (Mayr v Bäckerei [2008] CJEU). Appointments before implantation are not covered by s.55, though they may be covered by general sex discrimination protections under EqA 2010

Treating ante-natal absences as part of the sickness/absence record — ante-natal care appointments are a statutory right and must NOT be recorded as sickness absence, unauthorised absence, or counted against the employee in attendance management procedures. Any adverse attendance recording connected to ante-natal care is a detriment under s.47C

sections.title: Time off for dependants
sections.summary: An employee is entitled to be permitted by their employer to take a REASONABLE amount of UNPAID time off during working hours in order to take action which is NECESSARY to deal with certain emergency or unforeseen situations involving dependants. This is a DAY-ONE right — no qualifying period. The right covers five qualifying circumstances: (1) dependant falls ill, gives birth, is injured or assaulted; (2) arrangements for provision of care for a dependant are unexpectedly disrupted or terminated; (3) death of a dependant; (4) unexpected disruption or termination of care arrangements for a dependant who is ill or injured; (5) unexpected incident involving a child at school. The right is for EMERGENCIES ONLY — not for ongoing care arrangements (Qua v John Ford Morrison [2003]). Dismissal for taking time off under s.57A is automatically unfair (s.99(3)(f)). Separate from Carer's Leave (ss.80J-N, Carer's Leave Act 2023, in force 6 April 2024) which covers planned, non-emergency caring for dependants with long-term care needs.
sections.text:
(1) An employee is entitled to be permitted by his employer to take a reasonable amount of time off during the employee's working hours in order to take action which is necessary—

(a) to provide assistance on an occasion when a dependant falls ill, gives birth or is injured or assaulted,

(b) to make arrangements for the provision of care for a dependant who is ill or injured,

(c) in consequence of the death of a dependant,

(d) because of the unexpected disruption or termination of arrangements for the care of a dependant, or

(e) to deal with an incident which involves a child of the employee and which occurs unexpectedly in a period during which an educational establishment which the child attends is responsible for him.

(2) Subsection (1) does not apply unless the employee—

(a) tells his employer the reason for his absence as soon as reasonably practicable, and

(b) except where paragraph (a) cannot be complied with until after the employee has returned to work, tells his employer for how long he expects to be absent.

(3) In this section "dependant" means, in relation to an employee—

(a) a spouse or civil partner,

(b) a child,

(c) a parent,

(d) a person who lives in the same household as the employee, otherwise than by reason of being his employee, tenant, lodger or boarder.

(4) In subsection (1)(a) or (b) "dependant" also means any person who reasonably relies on the employee—

(a) for assistance on an occasion when the person falls ill or is injured or assaulted, or

(b) to make arrangements for the provision of care in the event of illness or injury.

(5) In subsection (1)(d) "dependant" includes, in addition to the persons mentioned in subsection (3), any person who reasonably relies on the employee to make arrangements for the provision of care.
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 57A creates a RIGHT to time off — not a discretion. The employer must PERMIT the time off if the statutory conditions are met. Three conditions must be satisfied: (1) one of the five qualifying circumstances exists; (2) the time off is for action that is NECESSARY (not merely convenient); (3) the amount of time off is REASONABLE (usually hours or 1-2 days — Qua v John Ford Morrison [2003]). The right is for dealing with emergencies and making care arrangements — NOT for providing ongoing care. Once the immediate crisis is dealt with and arrangements are in place, the right is exhausted.

fiveCircumstances.a: Dependant falls ill, gives birth, is injured or assaulted — covers a wide range of emergencies from sudden illness to assault
fiveCircumstances.b: Making arrangements for care of an ill or injured dependant — the emphasis is on ARRANGING care, not on providing care directly
fiveCircumstances.c: Death of a dependant — covers funeral arrangements and immediate bereavement (separate from the new bereavement leave under ERA 2025)
fiveCircumstances.d: Unexpected disruption or termination of care arrangements — childminder fails to arrive, nursery closes unexpectedly, care home can no longer accommodate
fiveCircumstances.e: Unexpected incident involving a child at school — child injured, excluded, or involved in an incident during school hours

whoIsADependant.coreDefinition: s.57A(3): spouse/civil partner, child, parent, or any person living in the same household (excluding employees, tenants, lodgers, boarders)
whoIsADependant.extendedDefinition: For circumstances (a) and (b): also includes any person who REASONABLY RELIES on the employee for assistance when ill/injured, or to make care arrangements (s.57A(4)). For circumstance (d): also includes any person who reasonably relies on the employee to arrange care (s.57A(5)).
whoIsADependant.practicalNote: The extended definition means the right can cover elderly neighbours, friends, or non-household members who depend on the employee for care. It is not limited to family members.

notificationObligation.requirement: The employee must: (a) tell the employer the REASON for the absence as soon as reasonably practicable; AND (b) tell the employer how long they expect to be absent. Exception: if the employee cannot comply with (a) until after returning to work, they must do so on their return.
notificationObligation.practicalNote: The notification does not have to be in any particular form — a phone call, text, or email is sufficient. The employee must give fresh notice if the absence needs to be extended (EAT guidance). Failure to notify does not retrospectively make the absence unauthorised — but the employer may not be required to permit time off if no notification has been given.

whatIsReasonable.principle: The legislation does not specify a maximum period. The tribunal assesses reasonableness on the facts. In Qua v John Ford Morrison [2003], the EAT stated that in most cases, no more than a few hours or, at most, one or possibly two days would be regarded as reasonable. The purpose is to deal with the immediate crisis and arrange care — not to provide extended care.
whatIsReasonable.notRelevant: The disruption or inconvenience caused to the employer's BUSINESS is NOT a relevant factor in assessing reasonableness (Qua v John Ford Morrison [2003]). The assessment focuses on the employee's needs, not the employer's operational impact.

emergencyOnly.principle: The right is for EMERGENCIES and UNFORESEEN situations only. It is not intended for planned absences, ongoing care, or regular childcare responsibilities. An employee who takes repeated time off for the same ongoing situation (e.g., a child with a chronic condition) may exceed the scope of s.57A (Qua v John Ford Morrison [2003]).
emergencyOnly.ongoingCare: For planned, non-emergency caring responsibilities, the employee should use: Carer's Leave (ss.80J-N, Carer's Leave Act 2023 — 1 week unpaid per year, in force 6 April 2024); parental leave (s.76 — 18 weeks unpaid per child); annual leave; or any contractual leave arrangements.

protections.detriment: s.47C(2)(c) ERA 1996 — an employee has the right not to be subjected to any detriment by the employer for taking time off under s.57A. A disciplinary warning, reduced performance rating, or any other disadvantage connected to the exercise of the right is a detriment.
protections.dismissal: s.99(3)(f) ERA 1996 — dismissal for taking time off under s.57A is AUTOMATICALLY UNFAIR. No qualifying period. This applies even if the employer characterises the dismissal as being for a different reason (e.g., misconduct, poor attendance) — if the real reason is connected to the exercise of the s.57A right, the dismissal is automatically unfair (Clarke v Credit Resource Solutions [2011]).
protections.complaint: s.57B ERA 1996 — the employee may present a complaint to the tribunal that the employer has unreasonably refused to permit time off. The tribunal may make a declaration and order compensation.

distinctionFromCarersLeave.provision: ss.80J-N ERA 1996 (inserted by Carer's Leave Act 2023, in force 6 April 2024)
distinctionFromCarersLeave.distinction: s.57A (dependants) is for EMERGENCIES — unforeseen, immediate, short-duration. Carer's Leave (ss.80J-N) is for PLANNED caring — 1 week unpaid per year for employees who care for a dependant with a long-term care need. The two rights are SEPARATE and serve DIFFERENT purposes. An employee may use s.57A for an emergency involving the same dependant for whom they also take Carer's Leave. There is no interaction or set-off between the two rights.
distinctionFromCarersLeave.carersLeaveDetails: Carer's Leave: day-one right, 1 week per year (can be taken in half-days), unpaid, for dependants with long-term care needs (physical/mental illness or disability expected to last 3+ months, or disability under EqA 2010). The employee must give notice. The leave is for PROVIDING care — unlike s.57A which is for dealing with emergencies.

versions.text: [s.57A was inserted by the Employment Relations Act 1999 s.8, Sch.4 Part II, in force 15 December 1999. Minor amendments: Civil Partnership Act 2004 (added civil partner to the definition of dependant). The core provision is unchanged.]
versions.amendedBy: Inserted by Employment Relations Act 1999 s.8, Sch.4 Pt.II; amended by Civil Partnership Act 2004 s.261, Sch.27 para.151
versions.policyRationale: s.57A implemented the Parental Leave Directive (96/34/EC) force majeure provisions. The UK chose to provide an unpaid right to time off for emergencies, rather than paid leave. The right was designed to enable employees to deal with unforeseen domestic emergencies without risking their employment.

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 introduces a new right to unpaid BEREAVEMENT LEAVE (including pregnancy loss before 24 weeks), expected to come into force in 2027. This will interact with s.57A(1)(c) (death of a dependant) — the new bereavement leave will provide a longer, more structured entitlement than the emergency time off under s.57A. The employee may use s.57A for the immediate crisis and then transition to bereavement leave for the extended period.
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: Bereavement leave: expected 2027. s.57A itself is NOT amended by ERA 2025.
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: LOW for s.57A directly. The interaction with bereavement leave creates a two-stage entitlement: s.57A for the immediate crisis + bereavement leave for the extended period.

refs: ERA 1996 s.57A (this section — time off for dependants)
ERA 1996 s.57B (complaint to tribunal for unreasonable refusal)
ERA 1996 s.47C(2)(c) (detriment for taking time off — prohibited)
ERA 1996 s.99(3)(f) (dismissal for taking time off — automatically unfair)
ERA 1996 ss.80J-N (Carer's Leave — in force 6 April 2024, separate from s.57A)
Employment Relations Act 1999 s.8, Sch.4 Pt.II (inserted s.57A — 15 December 1999)
Carer's Leave Act 2023 (inserted ss.80J-N — 1 week unpaid for long-term care needs)
Carer's Leave Regulations 2024 (SI 2024/XXX — detailed scheme for ss.80J-N)
ERA 2025 (bereavement leave — interaction with s.57A(1)(c) death of dependant)

leadingCases.name: Qua v John Ford Morrison Solicitors
leadingCases.principle: The right under s.57A is for EMERGENCY situations only — not for ongoing care arrangements. In most cases, no more than a few hours or, at most, one or possibly two days would be regarded as reasonable. The disruption to the employer's business is NOT a relevant factor in assessing reasonableness. The employee who took 17 absences over a year for a child's ongoing medical condition was exceeding the scope of s.57A.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that s.57A was intended for emergency situations and not for providing ongoing care. The tribunal set out guidelines: the right is to deal with the immediate crisis and make arrangements, not to provide extended care. In most cases, a reasonable amount of time off would be a few hours or 1-2 days.
leadingCases.significance: THE foundational EAT authority on s.57A. Establishes the emergency-only principle, the 'hours/1-2 days' guideline for reasonableness, and the irrelevance of employer disruption. Must be read alongside RBS v Harrison (which expanded the meaning of 'unexpected').

leadingCases.name: Royal Bank of Scotland v Harrison
leadingCases.principle: 'Unexpected' does not mean 'sudden'. A disruption to care arrangements is 'unexpected' at the time the employee LEARNS OF IT, even if the employee then has advance warning before the absence. An employee who learned two weeks in advance that childcare would fail, and who took all reasonable steps to find alternative arrangements, was entitled to use s.57A when those steps failed.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that 'unexpected' in s.57A(1)(d) means unexpected when the employee learns of the disruption — not unexpected in the sense of sudden or immediate. The employee who knew in advance that childcare would fail, and had unsuccessfully tried to arrange alternatives, was entitled to rely on s.57A.
leadingCases.significance: Expanded the scope of 'unexpected' significantly. Important because it means employees who know IN ADVANCE that care will fail — but who have taken reasonable steps to find alternatives — can still rely on s.57A. The test is whether the disruption was unexpected WHEN LEARNED ABOUT.

leadingCases.name: Clarke v Credit Resource Solutions Ltd
leadingCases.principle: Dismissal for taking time off under s.57A is automatically unfair under s.99(3)(f), even if the employer characterises the dismissal as being for a different reason (e.g., refusing a reasonable instruction, unacceptable behaviour). If the real reason for the dismissal is connected to the exercise of the s.57A right, the dismissal is automatically unfair.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT upheld the tribunal's finding that the employee's dismissal was automatically unfair. The employee was late to work because childcare arrangements failed. When he refused to sign a 'late form' (which he perceived as penalising him for taking dependant leave), a heated debate ensued and he was dismissed. The employer argued the dismissal was for refusing instructions and unacceptable behaviour — but the tribunal found the real reason was connected to taking time off for dependants.
leadingCases.significance: Demonstrates that employers cannot disguise a dismissal connected to s.57A as being for conduct or insubordination. The tribunal will examine the REAL reason. If the real reason is connected to the exercise of the s.57A right, the dismissal is automatically unfair — regardless of how the employer labels it.

leadingCases.name: Cortest Ltd v O'Toole
leadingCases.principle: Taking a month off work to care for a child when childcare arrangements broke down exceeded the scope of s.57A. The right is to deal with the immediate crisis and arrange care — not to provide extended care. After a reasonable initial period (hours/1-2 days), the employee must make alternative arrangements.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT confirmed that taking a month off to provide ongoing childcare was not covered by s.57A. The right is time-limited to dealing with the emergency and making arrangements — not providing the care itself over an extended period.
leadingCases.significance: Establishes the outer boundary of s.57A — one month is clearly excessive. Read alongside Qua (hours/1-2 days is the normal range). For extended caring needs, the employee must use Carer's Leave (ss.80J-N), parental leave, annual leave, or other arrangements.

leadingCases.name: Truelove v Safeway Stores plc
leadingCases.principle: The employee must give the employer notice of the reason for absence 'as soon as reasonably practicable' under s.57A(2). Failure to give proper notice does not retrospectively make the absence a misconduct issue — but the employer is not required to treat the absence as protected under s.57A if no notification was given.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the notification requirement in s.57A(2) is a condition of the entitlement. The employee must tell the employer the reason for the absence as soon as reasonably practicable. If the employee fails to do so, the absence may not be protected under s.57A.
leadingCases.significance: Clarifies the notification requirement — it is a condition of the right, not a formality. Employers who discipline employees for failure to notify properly are on stronger ground than employers who discipline for the absence itself.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Time off for family and dependants
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance on the right to time off for dependants, including the scope of the right, the definition of dependant, and the employer's obligations.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Time off for family and dependants
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance on the right to unpaid time off for family emergencies.

commonErrors: Treating the right as discretionary — s.57A creates a RIGHT to time off, not a discretion. The employer must PERMIT the time off if the statutory conditions are met. Refusing time off that meets the conditions is an unreasonable refusal under s.57B and may lead to a tribunal claim

Not recognising that s.57A is a DAY-ONE right — no qualifying period applies. An employee on their first day of employment who receives a call that a dependant has been taken to hospital is entitled to take reasonable time off. The employer cannot refuse on the basis of short service

Assuming 'unexpected' means 'sudden' — RBS v Harrison [2009] clarifies that 'unexpected' means unexpected when the employee LEARNS OF IT. An employee who knew two weeks in advance that childcare would fail, and who took all reasonable steps to find alternatives, can still rely on s.57A when those steps failed. The test is when the disruption became known, not when the absence occurs

Allowing employees to use s.57A for ongoing care — the right is for EMERGENCIES ONLY. In most cases, a few hours or 1-2 days is the reasonable maximum (Qua v John Ford Morrison [2003]). A month off to provide childcare is clearly outside the scope (Cortest v O'Toole [2008]). For ongoing care, the employee should use Carer's Leave (ss.80J-N), parental leave, annual leave, or other arrangements

Not distinguishing s.57A from Carer's Leave (ss.80J-N) — these are SEPARATE rights serving DIFFERENT purposes. s.57A = emergency, unforeseen, short-duration, for making arrangements. Carer's Leave (in force 6 April 2024) = planned, 1 week per year, for dependants with long-term care needs (3+ months expected). The two rights do not interact or set off against each other

Penalising an employee for exercising the right — s.47C(2)(c) prohibits detriment for taking time off under s.57A. A disciplinary warning, adverse attendance record, reduced bonus, negative performance review, or any other disadvantage connected to the exercise of the right is unlawful. Even asking the employee to sign a 'late form' may constitute a detriment (Clarke v Credit Resource Solutions [2011])

Not knowing that dismissal for taking s.57A leave is AUTOMATICALLY UNFAIR — s.99(3)(f) makes dismissal for taking time off under s.57A automatically unfair with NO qualifying period. The employer cannot characterise the dismissal as being for another reason (misconduct, poor attitude) if the REAL reason is connected to the exercise of the s.57A right (Clarke v Credit Resource Solutions [2011])

Considering the impact on the employer's business when assessing reasonableness — the EAT in Qua v John Ford Morrison [2003] stated that the disruption or inconvenience caused to the employer's business is NOT a relevant factor in assessing whether the amount of time off is reasonable. The assessment focuses on the employee's needs and circumstances

Not requiring proper notification — s.57A(2) requires the employee to tell the employer the reason for absence as soon as reasonably practicable AND the expected duration. This is a condition of the right. An employee who fails to notify may lose the s.57A protection (Truelove v Safeway [2005]). Employers should ensure their policies make the notification requirement clear

Applying a rigid limit on the number of occasions — there is no statutory limit on how many times an employee can exercise the right. Each occasion is assessed on its own facts. However, repeated use for the same ongoing situation may exceed the scope of s.57A (Qua v John Ford Morrison [2003] — 17 occasions for the same child's chronic condition was outside the scope)

Not recognising the broad definition of 'dependant' — the definition extends beyond immediate family. Under s.57A(4), for circumstances (a) and (b), a dependant includes any person who REASONABLY RELIES on the employee for assistance or care arrangements. This can include elderly neighbours, friends, or other non-household members who depend on the employee

sections.title: Right to time off to look for work or arrange training
sections.summary: An employee who is given notice of dismissal by reason of redundancy is entitled to be permitted reasonable PAID time off during working hours to look for new employment or arrange training for future employment — provided the employee has been continuously employed for 2 years or more by the date on which the notice is due to expire. The time off is PAID at the appropriate hourly rate (s.53). This is the only time-off provision in the ERA that specifically applies in the redundancy context. The 2-year qualifying period (s.52(2)) is NOT changed by ERA 2025.
sections.text:
(1) An employee who is given notice of dismissal by reason of redundancy is entitled to be permitted by his employer to take reasonable time off during the employee's working hours before the end of his notice in order to—

(a) look for new employment, or

(b) make arrangements for training for future employment.

(2) An employee is not entitled to take time off under this section unless, on the date on which the notice is due to expire, he will have been (or would have been) continuously employed for a period of two years or more.
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 52 ensures that redundant employees with qualifying service can spend time during their notice period looking for new work or arranging training — without loss of pay. The right applies during the NOTICE PERIOD only (not before notice is given). The amount of time off must be REASONABLE — there is no statutory minimum or maximum. The right is PAID under s.53 at the appropriate hourly rate — but the maximum payment is 40% of a week's pay (s.53(5)).

paidRight.entitlement: Paid at the appropriate hourly rate for the period of absence. However, the total remuneration payable is CAPPED at 40% of a week's pay (s.53(5)). This means the maximum payment for time off under s.52 is 2/5 of a week's pay — regardless of how much time off is actually taken.
paidRight.practicalNote: The 40% cap means that, in practice, the financial entitlement is modest. For an employee earning at the £751 weekly cap (from April 2026), the maximum s.52 payment is £300.40. Many employers permit more generous time off as a matter of good practice.

qualifyingPeriod.period: 2 years' continuous employment by the date the notice expires
qualifyingPeriod.era2025: NOT changed by ERA 2025. The s.52 qualifying period remains at 2 years — the same as the redundancy payment qualifying period (s.155).

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 does not amend s.52. The 2-year qualifying period and the right itself are unchanged.

refs: ERA 1996 s.52 (this section)
ERA 1996 s.53 (remuneration — paid at appropriate hourly rate, capped at 40% of week's pay)
ERA 1996 s.54 (complaint to tribunal for unreasonable refusal)
ERA 1996 s.155 (2-year qualifying period for redundancy pay — same qualifying period)

leadingCases.name: Dutton v Hawker Siddeley Aviation Ltd
leadingCases.principle: The employer cannot require the employee to provide proof of specific appointments or interviews as a condition of granting time off under s.52. The right is to time off to LOOK FOR work — not to attend confirmed interviews. The employee does not need to demonstrate they have a particular job opportunity lined up.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the right to time off to look for work does not require the employee to have specific appointments. The right covers the process of job searching, not just attending interviews.
leadingCases.significance: Broad interpretation — time off for general job searching, not just specific interviews.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Redundancy: your rights
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance including the right to time off during the notice period.

commonErrors: Not knowing the right is PAID — s.53 entitles the employee to be paid for the time off, though capped at 40% of a week's pay. Many employers assume the right is unpaid (like s.50 public duties) — it is not

Not recognising the 2-year qualifying period — s.52(2) requires 2 years' continuous employment by the notice expiry date. Employees with less than 2 years' service do not have the statutory right (though employers may permit time off as a matter of good practice)

Requiring proof of specific interviews — the right is to time off to LOOK FOR work, not to attend specific interviews (Dutton v Hawker Siddeley [1978]). The employee does not need to provide appointment letters or interview invitations

Not recognising that the right only applies during the NOTICE PERIOD — s.52 applies after the employer gives notice of dismissal by reason of redundancy. It does not apply before notice is given (e.g., during the consultation period)

Not knowing the 40% weekly pay cap on remuneration — s.53(5) caps the total payment at 40% of a week's pay. Even if the employee takes several days off, the maximum payment is modest. The cap applies to the TOTAL payment, not to each occasion
Assuming ERA 2025 changes s.52 — ERA 2025 does not amend s.52. The 2-year qualifying period, the right, and the 40% cap are all unchanged

sections.title: Introductory — continuous employment
sections.summary: Section 210 is the INTRODUCTORY provision for the continuous employment rules (Chapter I, Part XIV, ss.210–219). It establishes three foundational principles: (1) continuous employment is computed WEEK BY WEEK (Sunday to Saturday), but expressed in months and years for statutory purposes (s.210(3)); (2) any question about whether employment counts towards continuity is determined week by week (s.210(4)); (3) there is a STATUTORY PRESUMPTION of continuity — a person's employment during any period shall, unless the contrary is shown, be PRESUMED to have been continuous (s.210(5)). This presumption places the burden on the EMPLOYER to prove that continuity was broken. Every qualifying period in the ERA 1996 depends on these rules: s.108 (unfair dismissal — 6 months from January 2027), s.155 (redundancy pay — 2 years), s.86 (notice — years of service), s.1 (statement of particulars — day one but must state start date of continuous employment).
sections.text:
(1) References in any provision of this Act to a period of continuous employment are (unless provision is expressly made to the contrary) to a period computed in accordance with this Chapter.

(2) [Repealed]

(3) In any provision of this Act which refers to a period of continuous employment expressed in months or years—

(a) a month means a calendar month, and

(b) a year means a year of twelve months.

(4) In computing an employee's period of continuous employment for the purposes of any provision of this Act, any question—

(a) whether the employee's employment is of a kind counting towards a period of continuous employment, or

(b) whether periods (consecutive or otherwise) are to be treated as forming a single period of continuous employment,

shall be determined week by week; but where it is necessary to compute the length of an employee's period of employment it shall be computed in months and years of twelve months in accordance with section 211.

(5) A person's employment during any period shall, unless the contrary is shown, be presumed to have been continuous.
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 210 is the FRAMEWORK provision. It establishes HOW continuity is assessed (week by week, Sunday to Saturday) and creates the PRESUMPTION of continuity (s.210(5)). The presumption is critically important: in any dispute, the employee's employment is PRESUMED continuous. The employer bears the BURDEN OF PROOF to show that continuity was broken. This means that gaps, ambiguities, and unclear periods are resolved in the employee's favour.

statutoryPresumption.text: A person's employment during any period shall, unless the contrary is shown, be presumed to have been continuous.
statutoryPresumption.effect: The burden of proof is on the EMPLOYER to demonstrate that continuity was broken. If the employer cannot prove a break, continuity is presumed. This applies in tribunal proceedings — the employee does not have to prove continuity; the employer must disprove it.
statutoryPresumption.practicalNote: The presumption is particularly important for employees with complex work histories — successive fixed-term contracts, seasonal work patterns, zero-hours arrangements, agency work transitioning to direct employment, and career breaks. The employer who argues that continuity was broken must point to a specific break of one clear week (Sunday to Saturday) with no contract in existence.

weekByWeek.computation: Continuity is determined WEEK BY WEEK. A 'week' means a week ending with Saturday (s.210, s.235). Each week is assessed independently — does a contract of employment exist during any part of that week? If yes, the week counts. If no contract exists during the ENTIRE week (Sunday to Saturday), the week does not count — and unless an exception applies (s.212(3)), the break ends continuity.
weekByWeek.startDate: The employee's continuous employment starts on the DAY they start work under their contract (s.211(1)). This includes the first day even if no work is performed on that day (e.g., the employee starts on a bank holiday or is inducted but does not perform substantive work).

whatDependsOnContinuity: Unfair dismissal qualifying period — s.108 (2 years, reducing to 6 months from 1 January 2027)
Statutory redundancy payment qualifying period — s.155 (2 years — unchanged by ERA 2025)
Statutory minimum notice — s.86 (1 week per year of continuous employment, max 12 weeks)
Written statement of particulars — s.1 (must state start date of continuous employment)
Statutory maternity pay — 26 weeks by the 15th week before EWC
Statutory paternity pay — 26 weeks (removed from April 2026 for leave, retained for pay)
Statutory redundancy payment calculation — s.162 (up to 20 years count)
Basic award calculation — s.119 (up to 20 years count)
Time off to look for work — s.52 (2 years)
Written reasons for dismissal — s.92 (2 years, with exceptions)

versions.text: [s.210 is substantively unchanged since enactment. s.210(2) (which related to minimum hours thresholds) was repealed — all employment now counts regardless of hours worked.]
versions.amendedBy: ERA 1996 as enacted; s.210(2) repealed (minimum hours threshold removed)

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 does not amend ss.210–219. The continuity rules are UNCHANGED. However, the reduction of the unfair dismissal qualifying period to 6 months (s.108) makes the continuity rules more important for a larger population of employees — employees with 6+ months' service will need to establish continuity to bring unfair dismissal claims.
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: MODERATE — continuity disputes will increase in volume as more employees become eligible for unfair dismissal protection from 6 months' service.

leadingCases.name: Secretary of State for Employment v Globe Elastic Thread Co Ltd
leadingCases.principle: The courts will look sceptically at arrangements designed to reset the continuity clock and reduce statutory liabilities. Where an employer dismisses and re-engages an employee shortly afterwards to avoid the qualifying period, the tribunal may find that the arrangement was designed to defeat the employee's statutory rights and that continuity was not genuinely broken.
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords examined the purpose and operation of the continuity provisions and held that tribunals should scrutinise arrangements that appear designed to prevent employees from accruing statutory rights.
leadingCases.significance: THE foundational UKHL authority on anti-avoidance in the continuity context. Employers who engineer breaks in employment to reset the clock face judicial scepticism — particularly where the break is short and the re-engagement is pre-planned.

leadingCases.name: Sweeney v J & S Henderson (Concessions) Ltd
leadingCases.principle: The s.210(5) presumption of continuity is a LEGAL presumption that applies in tribunal proceedings. The employer bears the evidential burden of proving that continuity was broken. If the employer cannot adduce evidence of a clear break, the presumption resolves the issue in the employee's favour.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT confirmed that the s.210(5) presumption places the burden on the employer. The employee does not have to prove continuity — the employer must disprove it.
leadingCases.significance: Confirms the practical operation of the s.210(5) presumption. Important for employees with complex work histories — the employer must prove the break, not the employee prove the continuity.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Continuous employment: what counts
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance on continuous employment including what breaks continuity and what preserves it.

commonErrors: Not knowing that the EMPLOYER bears the burden of proving a break in continuity — s.210(5) creates a statutory PRESUMPTION of continuity. In any dispute, the employee's employment is presumed continuous unless the employer proves otherwise. The employer must point to a specific break — gaps, ambiguities, and unclear periods are resolved in the employee's favour (Sweeney v Henderson [1999])

Assuming that any gap in employment breaks continuity — a break only occurs where there is ONE CLEAR WEEK (Sunday to Saturday) with NO contract of employment in existence. Shorter gaps (e.g., a few days between contracts) do NOT break continuity. The week runs Sunday to Saturday, not from the last day worked

Not recognising that continuity is computed WEEK BY WEEK — each week is assessed independently. The question is: was there a contract of employment in existence during ANY PART of that week? If yes, the week counts. Even working for one hour in a week preserves continuity for that week

Treating the removal of the minimum hours threshold as irrelevant — s.210(2) (which required a minimum number of hours) was repealed. ALL employment now counts towards continuity regardless of hours worked. Zero-hours workers, part-time workers, and irregular-hours workers all accrue continuity on the same basis as full-time workers

Not recognising how many statutory rights depend on continuity — unfair dismissal (s.108), redundancy pay (s.155), minimum notice (s.86), SMP/SPP qualifying periods, the basic award (s.119), the redundancy payment calculation (s.162), time off to look for work (s.52), and written reasons for dismissal (s.92) all depend on continuous employment. Getting the continuity calculation wrong affects multiple rights simultaneously

Engineering breaks to prevent accrual of qualifying service — employers who deliberately dismiss and re-engage employees to reset the continuity clock face judicial scepticism (Globe Elastic Thread [1980]). Tribunals will examine whether the arrangement was genuine or designed to defeat statutory rights

Not factoring in TUPE transfers — s.218(2) preserves continuity when a business or undertaking is transferred. The employee's continuous employment with the transferor counts as continuous employment with the transferee. The new employer inherits the full period of service

Assuming that ERA 2025 changes the continuity rules — ERA 2025 does NOT amend ss.210–219. The continuity rules are UNCHANGED. The reduction of the unfair dismissal qualifying period to 6 months makes continuity more important (more employees will need to establish it) but does not change how it is calculated

Not recording the start date of continuous employment correctly — s.1(4)(b) requires the written statement of particulars to include the date on which the employee's period of continuous employment began (taking into account any employment with a previous employer which counts towards that period). An incorrect start date can lead to incorrect qualifying period calculations

Not understanding the interaction between continuity and the 'relevant date' / 'effective date of termination' — continuity is calculated from the start date to the END date (EDT for unfair dismissal under s.97, relevant date for redundancy under s.145). The END date determines which qualifying period threshold must be met. Different end dates for different purposes can produce different results

Confusing continuity of employment (statutory, ss.210–219) with continuity of contract (common law) — statutory continuity can be preserved even where there is no contract in existence (e.g., during temporary cessation of work, s.212(3)(b)). Common law continuity requires a contract to be in existence. The statutory rules are more generous to the employee

sections.title: Weeks counting in computing period
sections.summary: Section 212 sets out which weeks count towards the employee's period of continuous employment. The core rule: any week during the whole or part of which the employee's relations with the employer are governed by a contract of employment COUNTS (s.212(1)). Where no contract exists, four statutory exceptions preserve continuity: (a) sickness or injury (max 26 weeks); (b) temporary cessation of work; (c) absence by arrangement or custom; (d) pregnancy or childbirth (max 26 weeks). These exceptions ensure that periods of absence from work do not break continuity where the absence is for a recognised reason. 'Temporary cessation of work' (s.212(3)(b)) is the most litigated exception — assessed retrospectively, looking back from the date of the employee's return to see whether the cessation was 'temporary' in nature.
sections.text:
(1) Any week during the whole or part of which an employee's relations with his employer are governed by a contract of employment counts in computing the employee's period of employment.

(2) [Repealed]

(3) Subject to subsection (4), any week (not within subsection (1)) during the whole or part of which an employee is—

(a) incapable of work in consequence of sickness or injury,

(b) absent from work on account of a temporary cessation of work,

(c) absent from work in circumstances such that, by arrangement or custom, he is regarded as continuing in the employment of his employer for any purpose, or

(d) absent from work wholly or partly because of pregnancy or childbirth,

counts in computing the employee's period of employment.

(4) Not more than twenty-six weeks count under subsection (3)(a) or (subject to subsection (2)) subsection (3)(d) between any periods falling under subsection (1).
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 212 answers the question: 'Does this week count towards continuous employment?' The answer depends on three layers: (1) s.212(1) — was there a contract of employment in existence? If yes, the week counts. Full stop. (2) If no contract existed, was the employee absent for one of the four reasons in s.212(3)? If yes, the week STILL counts. (3) If neither (1) nor (2) applies, the week does NOT count — and unless an industrial action exception applies (ss.215–216), the break ends continuity.

a_sickness.description: Incapable of work in consequence of sickness or injury
a_sickness.note: Up to 26 weeks of sickness or injury absence counts towards continuity, even where no contract exists during the absence. Beyond 26 weeks, the absence no longer counts — but it does not automatically BREAK continuity either (ss.213–214 provide that weeks which don't count don't break continuity in certain circumstances).

b_temporaryCessation.description: Absent from work on account of a temporary cessation of work
b_temporaryCessation.note: The most litigated exception. 'Temporary' is assessed RETROSPECTIVELY — looking back from the date of return (or the date the claim is brought) to determine whether the cessation was temporary in nature. In Flack v Kodak [1987], the Court of Appeal held that the mathematical approach (comparing the length of the gap with the length of the surrounding periods of employment) is the correct method for regular seasonal workers. For irregular patterns, a broader approach is taken, considering all the circumstances.
b_temporaryCessation.practicalNote: This exception is critical for seasonal workers, construction workers, and employees laid off during business downturns. A gap that was expected to be temporary at the time it occurred — and which proved to be temporary by the employee's return — is covered by s.212(3)(b).

c_arrangementOrCustom.description: Absent from work in circumstances such that, by arrangement or custom, the employee is regarded as continuing in employment for any purpose
c_arrangementOrCustom.note: This covers career breaks, sabbaticals, and agreed periods of absence where the parties regard the employment as continuing. The arrangement must exist AT THE TIME of the absence — it cannot be created retrospectively. 'Custom' means an established practice (e.g., an industry practice of seasonal breaks). In Booth v United States of America [1999], the EAT held that an arrangement to return to work after a gap was sufficient to preserve continuity under s.212(3)(c).
c_arrangementOrCustom.practicalNote: The key test is whether the parties REGARDED the employment as continuing. Evidence includes: agreed return dates, retention of benefits (e.g., continued pension contributions), company communications addressed to the employee during the absence, and keeping the employee on the HR system.

d_pregnancy.description: Absent from work wholly or partly because of pregnancy or childbirth
d_pregnancy.note: Up to 26 weeks of pregnancy or childbirth absence counts towards continuity. In practice, this is rarely the operative provision because statutory maternity leave preserves the contract of employment — so s.212(1) (contract in existence) applies. s.212(3)(d) is a safety net for situations where the contract has ended but the employee returns within 26 weeks.

industrialAction.principle: A week in which the employee takes part in a strike does NOT COUNT towards continuous employment — but it does NOT BREAK continuity either. The strike weeks are effectively 'frozen out' of the calculation. Similarly, a week in which the employee is locked out by the employer does not break continuity.

whatBreaksContinuity.oneWeekRule: A break of ONE CLEAR WEEK (Sunday to Saturday) with no contract of employment in existence and no s.212(3) exception applicable will break continuity. The week runs from Sunday to Saturday — not from the last day of one contract to the first day of the next.
whatBreaksContinuity.previousRedundancyPayment: s.219 provides that where a redundancy payment has been paid and the employee is re-employed (by the same or another employer), continuity is broken for REDUNDANCY PAYMENT purposes only. It does not break continuity for unfair dismissal purposes.

versions.text: [s.212 is substantively unchanged since enactment. s.212(2) (which related to minimum hours) was repealed. The four exceptions in s.212(3) remain as originally enacted.]
versions.amendedBy: ERA 1996 as enacted; s.212(2) repealed (minimum hours removed)

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 does not amend s.212. The rules for which weeks count towards continuity are UNCHANGED.
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: LOW — the rules themselves are unchanged, but their practical importance increases as more employees become eligible for unfair dismissal from 6 months' service.

ERA 1996 s.210 (introductory — framework and presumption)
ERA 1996 s.211 (start and end dates for the period)
ERA 1996 s.213–214 (weeks not counting — but not breaking continuity)
ERA 1996 s.216 (strikes — don't count but don't break)
ERA 1996 s.218 (change of employer — TUPE preserves continuity)
