leadingCases.name: Ford v Warwickshire County Council
leadingCases.principle: A seasonal worker employed on successive fixed-term contracts with gaps between them may have continuous employment if the gaps are covered by s.212(3)(b) (temporary cessation of work) or s.212(3)(c) (arrangement or custom). The s.210(5) presumption of continuity applies — the employer must prove the gap broke continuity.
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords held that a teacher employed on successive annual fixed-term contracts had continuous employment — the summer gaps were temporary cessations of work under s.212(3)(b). The presumption of continuity under s.210(5) applied.
leadingCases.significance: THE foundational UKHL authority on temporary cessation for seasonal/fixed-term workers. Establishes that the presumption of continuity resolves ambiguities in the employee's favour.

leadingCases.name: Flack v Kodak Ltd
leadingCases.principle: For regular seasonal workers, the 'mathematical approach' to temporary cessation is appropriate — comparing the length of the gap with the length of the surrounding periods of employment. If the gap is short relative to the working periods, the cessation is 'temporary'. For irregular patterns, a broader, multi-factor approach is taken.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that for a seasonal worker with a regular pattern, the correct approach was to compare the length of the break with the length of the working periods on either side. The employee worked for several months, was laid off for several months, then returned — the lay-off was temporary relative to the working periods.
leadingCases.significance: Establishes the mathematical/comparative approach to 'temporary cessation'. The length of the gap relative to the surrounding employment is the key metric. A gap that is short relative to the working periods is temporary; a gap that is long relative to the working periods may not be.

leadingCases.name: Booth v United States of America
leadingCases.principle: An arrangement between the employer and employee that the employee will return to work after a gap is sufficient to preserve continuity under s.212(3)(c). The arrangement must exist AT THE TIME of the absence — it cannot be created retrospectively after the employee returns.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that an arrangement between the parties that the employee would return after a period of absence was sufficient for s.212(3)(c). The continuity was preserved because both parties regarded the employment as continuing.
leadingCases.significance: Clarifies the 'arrangement' limb of s.212(3)(c). The arrangement must be mutual and must exist at the time of the absence. Career breaks, sabbaticals, and agreed absences with return arrangements are covered.

leadingCases.name: Curr v Marks & Spencer plc
leadingCases.principle: The 'arrangement or custom' exception in s.212(3)(c) does not require a legally enforceable agreement to return. It is sufficient that the parties understood and intended that the employment relationship would continue through the break. However, a bare agreement to consider re-engagement is not sufficient — there must be a mutual understanding that the employment continues.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that a career break scheme which entitled employees to priority in re-engagement was an 'arrangement' under s.212(3)(c). The parties regarded the employment as continuing through the break, even though there was no legally binding obligation to re-employ.
leadingCases.significance: Distinguishes between an arrangement to continue employment (preserves continuity) and a bare agreement to consider re-engagement (does not). Career break schemes that provide for return with continuity preserved are covered.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Continuous employment: what counts
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance on which absences preserve continuity and which break it.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Checking your employment rights
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance including continuous employment assessment.

commonErrors: Not knowing the four statutory exceptions that preserve continuity — s.212(3) lists four situations where weeks WITHOUT a contract still count: (a) sickness/injury (max 26 weeks); (b) temporary cessation of work (no limit); (c) arrangement or custom (no limit); (d) pregnancy/childbirth (max 26 weeks). Employers who assume any gap without a contract breaks continuity are wrong

Not understanding 'temporary cessation of work' — s.212(3)(b) is assessed RETROSPECTIVELY. The question is whether, looking back from the employee's return, the cessation was temporary. For regular seasonal patterns, the mathematical approach applies (Flack v Kodak [1987]): compare the gap with the surrounding employment periods. A gap that is short relative to the work periods is temporary

Not recognising the 'arrangement or custom' exception — s.212(3)(c) covers career breaks, sabbaticals, and agreed absences where the parties regard the employment as continuing. The arrangement must exist AT THE TIME of the absence (Booth v USA [1999]). A bare agreement to consider future re-employment is NOT sufficient (Curr v M&S [2003]) — there must be a mutual understanding that the relationship continues

Assuming that strike weeks break continuity — s.216 provides that strike weeks do NOT count towards continuous employment but they do NOT BREAK continuity either. The weeks are 'frozen out' — they don't add to the total but they don't reset the clock. Similarly, lock-out weeks do not break continuity

Not recognising the 26-week limit on sickness and pregnancy exceptions — s.212(4) caps the sickness (s.212(3)(a)) and pregnancy (s.212(3)(d)) exceptions at 26 weeks between periods of actual employment. Beyond 26 weeks, these exceptions no longer preserve continuity. However, 'temporary cessation' (s.212(3)(b)) and 'arrangement or custom' (s.212(3)(c)) have NO time limit

Not applying s.212(1) correctly — the core rule is simple: any week during the whole or part of which a contract of employment exists COUNTS. Even one day of employment in a week is sufficient. Zero-hours workers who work one shift in a week have continuity for that week. The contract need not be performed — its existence is enough

Confusing the 'one clear week' break with calendar days — a break only ends continuity if there is ONE CLEAR WEEK (Sunday to Saturday) with no contract and no s.212(3) exception. The week runs Sunday to Saturday, not from the last day worked. An employee whose contract ends on a Tuesday and who starts a new contract the following Monday has not had a clear week — continuity is preserved

Not recognising that TUPE transfers preserve continuity — s.218(2) provides that where a business is transferred, the employee's continuous employment with the transferor counts as continuous employment with the transferee. The new employer INHERITS the full service history. This applies to TUPE transfers, associated employer changes, and statutory successions

Not recognising that a previous redundancy payment breaks continuity for REDUNDANCY purposes only — s.219 provides that where a redundancy payment has been paid and the employee is re-employed, continuity is broken for REDUNDANCY PAYMENT purposes. It is NOT broken for UNFAIR DISMISSAL purposes. An employee who received a redundancy payment and was re-employed still has full continuity for unfair dismissal

Assuming that successive fixed-term contracts with gaps always break continuity — Ford v Warwickshire [1983] establishes that the s.210(5) presumption applies to successive fixed-term contracts. The gaps between contracts may be temporary cessations (s.212(3)(b)) or covered by arrangement (s.212(3)(c)). The employer must prove the gaps broke continuity — the presumption favours the employee

Not documenting the start date of continuous employment correctly — s.1(4)(b) requires the written statement of particulars to include the date on which the employee's continuous employment began, taking into account any previous employment that counts. An employer who fails to include previous service (e.g., after a TUPE transfer or from an associated employer) exposes itself to incorrect qualifying period calculations and potential EA 2002 s.38 penalties

parts.title: Part V — Protection from Suffering Detriment (Supplementary)

sections.title: Protected disclosures — detriment
sections.summary: A WORKER has the right not to be subjected to any detriment by any act, or any deliberate failure to act, by their employer done ON THE GROUND THAT the worker has made a protected disclosure. This is a WORKER right — broader than an employee right. It covers employees, agency workers, LLP members, NHS practitioners, police officers, and anyone who works under a contract to perform personally any work (s.43K). No qualifying period. UNCAPPED compensation. The causation test is 'on the ground that' — meaning the protected disclosure must have MATERIALLY INFLUENCED the detrimental treatment (more than a trivial influence) — Fecitt v NHS Manchester [2012] EWCA. This is a LOWER threshold than the s.103A test for dismissal (which requires the disclosure to be the 'reason or principal reason'). Individual co-workers and agents can be personally liable (s.47B(1A), inserted by ERRA 2013). The employer is vicariously liable for co-worker detriment (s.47B(1B)) unless it took 'all reasonable steps' to prevent it (s.47B(1D)).
sections.text:
(1) A worker has the right not to be subjected to any detriment by any act, or any deliberate failure to act, by his employer done on the ground that the worker has made a protected disclosure.

(1A) A worker ("W") has the right not to be subjected to any detriment by any act, or any deliberate failure to act, done—

(a) by another worker of W's employer in the course of that other worker's employment, or

(b) by an agent of W's employer with the employer's authority,

on the ground that W has made a protected disclosure.

(1B) Where a worker is subjected to detriment by anything done as mentioned in subsection (1A), that thing is treated as also done by the worker's employer.

(1C) For the purposes of subsection (1B), it is immaterial whether the thing is done with the knowledge or approval of the worker's employer.

(1D) In proceedings against W's employer in respect of anything alleged to have been done as mentioned in subsection (1A)(a), it is a defence for W's employer to show that the employer took all reasonable steps to prevent the other worker—

(a) from doing that thing, or

(b) from doing anything of that description.

(1E) A worker or agent of W's employer is not liable by reason of subsection (1A) for doing anything as mentioned in that subsection if—

(a) the worker or agent does that thing in reliance on a statement by the employer that doing it does not contravene this Act, and

(b) it is reasonable for the worker or agent to rely on the statement.

(2) This section does not apply where—

(a) the worker is an employee, and

(b) the detriment in question amounts to dismissal (within the meaning of Part X).
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 47B creates the DETRIMENT right for whistleblowing — parallel to s.103A which covers DISMISSAL. The critical differences: (1) s.47B is a WORKER right (broader than employee); (2) the causation test is LOWER — 'materially influenced' rather than 'principal reason'; (3) individual co-workers can be personally liable; (4) the employer has an 'all reasonable steps' defence. These differences make s.47B the more accessible claim for whistleblowers — particularly those who have not been dismissed but have suffered adverse treatment short of dismissal.

causationTest.test: 'On the ground that' — the protected disclosure must have MATERIALLY INFLUENCED the employer's treatment of the worker, in the sense of being more than a trivial influence (Fecitt v NHS Manchester [2012] EWCA Civ 1190)
causationTest.comparedToS103A: s.103A (dismissal) requires the disclosure to be the 'reason or principal reason' for the dismissal — a HIGHER threshold. Under s.47B, the disclosure need only be A material factor, not THE principal factor. This means it is easier to succeed on a s.47B detriment claim than on a s.103A dismissal claim.
causationTest.burdenOfProof: Under s.48(2), once the worker establishes that they made a protected disclosure and suffered a detriment, the burden shifts to the EMPLOYER to prove the ground on which the act or failure to act was done. The employer must prove that the protected disclosure played no more than a trivial part in the treatment. If the employer cannot discharge this burden, the claim succeeds.
causationTest.separableConduct: The employer may argue that the detriment was caused by the MANNER of the disclosure (e.g., aggressive or insubordinate behaviour) rather than the disclosure itself. This is known as the 'separable conduct' defence — the employer must show that the detrimental treatment was genuinely caused by the worker's conduct, not by the fact of the disclosure (Martin v Devonshires Solicitors [2011]).

workerRight.principle: s.47B applies to WORKERS — a broader category than employees. The definition of 'worker' for whistleblowing purposes is extended by s.43K to include: employees, agency workers, homeworkers, NHS practitioners (GPs, dentists, pharmacists), police officers, and any individual who works under a contract to personally perform work or services. LLP members are also covered.
workerRight.notCovered: Genuinely self-employed individuals who are not under a contract to personally perform work, and volunteers.
workerRight.contrast: s.103A (auto-unfair dismissal for whistleblowing) applies to EMPLOYEES only. An agency worker who is not an employee cannot claim auto-unfair dismissal under s.103A but CAN claim detriment under s.47B.

coWorkerLiability.provision: s.47B(1A), inserted by Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013
coWorkerLiability.principle: A worker has the right not to be subjected to detriment by another worker of the same employer (in the course of that worker's employment) or by an agent of the employer (with the employer's authority). This creates INDIVIDUAL LIABILITY for co-workers — a manager who retaliates against a whistleblower can be personally sued.
coWorkerLiability.vicarousLiability: s.47B(1B)–(1C) — the employer is treated as having done the detrimental act, regardless of whether the employer knew about or approved it. The employer is vicariously liable for co-worker detriment.
coWorkerLiability.allReasonableSteps: s.47B(1D) — the employer has a defence if it can show it took ALL REASONABLE STEPS to prevent the co-worker from doing the detrimental act. This mirrors the discrimination law defence. Evidence of anti-retaliation policies, whistleblowing training, and effective complaint procedures will be relevant.
coWorkerLiability.relianceOnStatement: s.47B(1E) — a co-worker or agent is not liable if they relied on a statement by the employer that the action did not contravene the Act, and it was reasonable to rely on that statement.

dismissalException.principle: s.47B does NOT apply where the worker is an employee and the detriment amounts to DISMISSAL (within the meaning of Part X). Employees who are dismissed must bring their claim under s.103A (auto-unfair dismissal), not s.47B.
dismissalException.timisVOsipov: HOWEVER — the Court of Appeal in Timis v Osipov [2019] IRLR 52 held that a dismissal carried out by a CO-WORKER can constitute a detriment under s.47B(1A), and the dismissal exception in s.47B(2) does not block this claim. This means the employer can be vicariously liable under s.47B(1B) for a 'detriment of dismissal' caused by a co-worker — even where the employee also has a s.103A claim. This is tactically significant because the s.47B causation test is lower (material influence vs principal reason).
dismissalException.practicalNote: In practice, employees who are dismissed for whistleblowing will often plead BOTH s.103A (auto-unfair dismissal — principal reason test) AND s.47B(1A) (detriment of dismissal by co-worker — material influence test). The s.47B route provides a lower evidential threshold.

postEmploymentDetriment.principle: s.47B protection can extend to detriment suffered AFTER the employment relationship has ended, provided the detriment is sufficiently connected to the employment relationship. This includes: negative references, post-termination communications, blacklisting, and interference with new employment.
postEmploymentDetriment.dayVLewisham: In Day v Lewisham and Greenwich NHS Trust [2025], the EAT confirmed that post-employment detriment claims fall within s.47B where the detriment is sufficiently connected to the former employment. References and post-employment communications can attract liability if materially influenced by protected disclosures.

whatIsDetriment.principle: 'Detriment' is not defined in the ERA 1996. The test is whether a reasonable worker would or might take the view that the treatment was to their disadvantage in the circumstances (Shamoon v Chief Constable of the Royal Ulster Constabulary [2003] UKHL 11). It includes any disadvantage — financial, career, reputational, or psychological.
examples: Demotion, transfer, or change of duties
Exclusion from meetings, projects, or information
Bullying, harassment, or hostile treatment by colleagues
Failure to promote or consider for opportunities
Reduction in pay, bonus, or benefits
Suspension (Gogay v Hertfordshire CC [2000] — suspension itself can be a detriment)
Deliberate failure to investigate the disclosure

versions.text: [s.47B was inserted by PIDA 1998. s.47B(1A)–(1E) (co-worker and agent liability) were inserted by ERRA 2013 s.19, in force 25 June 2013. The core right in s.47B(1) is unchanged since 1998.]
versions.amendedBy: Inserted by Public Interest Disclosure Act 1998; s.47B(1A)–(1E) inserted by Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 s.19

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 adds s.43B(1)(da) — sexual harassment as a qualifying disclosure — from 6 April 2026. This means workers who disclose information about sexual harassment can bring s.47B detriment claims if they suffer adverse treatment for making the disclosure. Additionally, the Government has announced a review of the whistleblowing framework following the July 2025 Whistleblowing Law Review. Further reforms may be expected in 2026/2027.
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: s.43B(1)(da) sexual harassment disclosure: 6 April 2026. Whistleblowing framework review: consultation expected 2026.
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: MODERATE — the addition of sexual harassment as a qualifying disclosure category expands the scope of s.47B. Workers who report sexual harassment (whether experienced or witnessed) will have explicit whistleblowing detriment protection from April 2026.

leadingCases.name: Fecitt v NHS Manchester
leadingCases.principle: The causation test under s.47B is 'material influence' — the protected disclosure must have been more than a TRIVIAL influence on the employer's treatment of the worker. This is a LOWER threshold than the s.103A 'principal reason' test. Once the worker establishes a prima facie case (disclosure + detriment), the burden shifts to the employer under s.48(2) to prove the ground for its action.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the correct causation test under s.47B is whether the protected disclosure materially influenced (in the sense of being more than a trivial influence) the employer's treatment of the whistleblower. The employer must prove on the balance of probabilities that the disclosure did not materially influence the treatment.
leadingCases.significance: THE foundational EWCA authority on the s.47B causation test. Establishes 'material influence' as the standard — lower than 'principal reason' (s.103A). Also confirms the burden reversal under s.48(2). The most important s.47B authority.

leadingCases.name: Timis v Osipov
leadingCases.principle: A dismissal carried out by a CO-WORKER can constitute a detriment under s.47B(1A), and the dismissal exception in s.47B(2) does not block this 'detriment of dismissal' claim. The employer can be vicariously liable under s.47B(1B) for a co-worker's decision to dismiss the whistleblower — even where the employee also has a s.103A claim.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal, albeit reluctantly, held that the s.47B(2) dismissal exception only blocks claims under s.47B(1) (employer detriment), NOT claims under s.47B(1A) (co-worker detriment). A dismissal decision made by a co-worker is a detriment by a co-worker — and the employer is vicariously liable under s.47B(1B).
leadingCases.significance: Created the 'detriment of dismissal' claim route via s.47B(1A). Tactically critical because the s.47B causation test (material influence) is lower than the s.103A test (principal reason). Employees dismissed for whistleblowing now routinely plead both routes.

leadingCases.name: Shamoon v Chief Constable of the Royal Ulster Constabulary
leadingCases.principle: The test for 'detriment' is whether a reasonable worker would or might take the view that the treatment was to their disadvantage in the circumstances. It is not necessary to show financial loss or material harm — any disadvantage is sufficient.
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords held that detriment means being put at a disadvantage. The test is objective but viewed from the perspective of the reasonable worker. An unjustified sense of grievance is not sufficient, but any treatment that puts the worker at a genuine disadvantage is a detriment.
leadingCases.significance: THE foundational UKHL authority on the meaning of 'detriment'. Applied across both discrimination and whistleblowing law. Establishes the broad, worker-perspective test.

leadingCases.name: Martin v Devonshires Solicitors
leadingCases.principle: The employer may argue that the detriment was caused by the MANNER of the disclosure (e.g., the worker's behaviour in making it) rather than the FACT of the disclosure itself. This is the 'separable conduct' defence. If the detrimental treatment was genuinely caused by the worker's conduct rather than the disclosure, the s.47B claim fails.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that where the employer can show that the detrimental treatment was caused by something genuinely separable from the protected disclosure itself — such as the manner in which the worker raised the concern (e.g., abusively, in breach of confidentiality) — the claim may not succeed.
leadingCases.significance: Establishes the 'separable conduct' principle — the employer can distinguish between the act of disclosing (protected) and the manner of disclosing (potentially not protected). Important because employers frequently argue that the worker was disciplined for their behaviour, not for whistleblowing.

leadingCases.name: Day v Lewisham and Greenwich NHS Trust
leadingCases.principle: s.47B protection can extend to detriment suffered AFTER the employment relationship has ended, provided the detriment is sufficiently connected to the former employment. Post-employment statements, references, and communications can attract liability if materially influenced by protected disclosures.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the s.47B protections are not limited to acts occurring during the employment relationship. Where the detriment is sufficiently connected to the former employment, a post-employment detriment claim is within the scope of s.47B.
leadingCases.significance: Extends s.47B protection beyond the employment relationship. Important for whistleblowers who suffer retaliation after leaving — negative references, blacklisting, and interference with new employment are within scope.

leadingCases.name: Jesudason v Alder Hey Children's NHS Foundation Trust
leadingCases.principle: A deliberate failure to act can constitute a detriment under s.47B. The employer's failure to investigate a disclosure, failure to take action on the concerns raised, or failure to protect the whistleblower from retaliation can all be detriments — provided the failure is 'deliberate' (conscious decision not to act, not mere oversight).
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal confirmed that 'deliberate failure to act' within s.47B(1) includes a conscious decision by the employer not to do something — such as failing to investigate a disclosure or failing to protect the whistleblower. An inadvertent omission is not a deliberate failure.
leadingCases.significance: Clarifies the 'deliberate failure to act' limb of s.47B. Important because many whistleblowers suffer detriment not through positive acts but through the employer's failure to act — failing to investigate, failing to protect, failing to address the concerns raised.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Whistleblowing for employees
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance on whistleblowing rights including the right not to be subjected to detriment.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Whistleblowing at work
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance on whistleblowing protections for workers and employers.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Whistleblowing advice

commonErrors: Applying the s.103A causation test to s.47B claims — the causation tests are DIFFERENT. s.47B uses the 'material influence' test (was the disclosure more than a trivial influence on the treatment?). s.103A uses the 'principal reason' test (was the disclosure THE reason or principal reason for the dismissal?). The s.47B test is LOWER — it is easier to prove that a disclosure materially influenced detrimental treatment than to prove it was the principal reason for dismissal (Fecitt v NHS Manchester [2012])

Not recognising that s.47B is a WORKER right — s.47B applies to workers, not just employees. This includes agency workers, LLP members, NHS practitioners, police officers, and anyone under a contract to personally perform work (s.43K). An agency worker who suffers detriment for whistleblowing has a s.47B claim even though they cannot claim auto-unfair dismissal under s.103A (which is an employee-only right)

Not knowing about individual co-worker liability — s.47B(1A) (inserted by ERRA 2013) allows the whistleblower to claim against INDIVIDUAL co-workers and agents who subjected them to detriment. A manager who retaliates can be personally sued. The employer is also vicariously liable (s.47B(1B)) regardless of knowledge or approval (s.47B(1C))

Assuming the 'all reasonable steps' defence is easy to establish — s.47B(1D) provides a defence if the employer took ALL reasonable steps to prevent the co-worker detriment. The employer must demonstrate: anti-retaliation policies, whistleblowing training, effective investigation and complaint procedures, and that it took action when retaliation was identified. A paper policy is not enough — the employer must show active implementation

Not recognising the dismissal exception workaround (Timis v Osipov) — s.47B(2) prevents employees from using s.47B for dismissal. BUT Timis v Osipov [2019] EWCA held that a dismissal by a CO-WORKER is a detriment under s.47B(1A) and the s.47B(2) exception does not block it. Employees now routinely plead both s.103A (principal reason) and s.47B(1A) (material influence) — giving them two chances at the lower threshold

Not understanding the burden reversal — under s.48(2), once the worker proves they made a protected disclosure and suffered a detriment, the burden shifts to the EMPLOYER to prove the ground for its action. The employer must prove that the disclosure played no more than a trivial part. If the employer cannot discharge this burden, the claim succeeds. This is more favourable to the claimant than the general civil litigation standard

Confusing 'separable conduct' with the disclosure itself — an employer may argue that the detrimental treatment was caused by the MANNER of the disclosure, not the fact of it (Martin v Devonshires [2011]). However, this defence is narrow — the employer must show a genuinely separable reason that is distinct from the protected activity. Courts scrutinise this defence carefully

Not recognising that a 'deliberate failure to act' is a detriment — s.47B covers not just positive acts but also deliberate failures to act. An employer who consciously fails to investigate a disclosure, fails to protect the whistleblower from retaliation, or fails to act on the concerns raised may be liable (Jesudason v Alder Hey [2020] EWCA). The failure must be 'deliberate' — a conscious decision, not mere oversight

Not considering post-employment detriment — s.47B protection extends beyond the employment relationship. Negative references, post-employment communications, blacklisting, and interference with new employment can all be detriments if materially influenced by the protected disclosure (Day v Lewisham [2025] EAT). Former employers must consider the impact of any post-termination actions

Not recognising that ERA 2025 adds sexual harassment as a qualifying disclosure — from 6 April 2026, s.43B(1)(da) makes sexual harassment a qualifying disclosure in its own right. Workers who disclose information about sexual harassment (whether experienced by them or by others) will have explicit s.47B detriment protection. This is a significant expansion of the whistleblowing framework

Treating s.47B as a lesser claim than s.103A — s.47B is often tactically MORE valuable than s.103A. The causation test is lower (material influence vs principal reason). Compensation is uncapped in both claims. s.47B covers detriments short of dismissal that s.103A does not reach. And since Timis v Osipov, s.47B(1A) can even cover dismissal via the co-worker route. For whistleblowers, s.47B is the primary claim — s.103A is the dismissal-specific complement

sections.title: Meaning of 'protected disclosure'
sections.summary: In the ERA 1996, a 'protected disclosure' means a qualifying disclosure (as defined by s.43B) which is made by a worker in accordance with any of sections 43C to 43H. This is the GATEWAY definition — it brings together the TWO requirements for whistleblowing protection: (1) the disclosure must be a QUALIFYING DISCLOSURE under s.43B (information that in the worker's reasonable belief is in the public interest and tends to show one of the seven categories of wrongdoing); AND (2) the disclosure must be made to an APPROPRIATE PERSON under ss.43C–43H (employer, legal adviser, Minister, prescribed person, or wider disclosure). BOTH requirements must be satisfied for the disclosure to attract the protections of s.47B (detriment) and s.103A (dismissal).
sections.text: In this Act a "protected disclosure" means a qualifying disclosure (as defined by section 43B) which is made by a worker in accordance with any of sections 43C to 43H.
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 43A is deliberately simple — one sentence. It creates the compound concept of 'protected disclosure' by requiring BOTH a qualifying disclosure (WHAT is disclosed — s.43B) AND an appropriate method of disclosure (TO WHOM it is disclosed — ss.43C–43H). A disclosure that meets the s.43B test but is made to the wrong person is NOT a protected disclosure. A disclosure made to the right person but which does not meet the s.43B test is NOT a protected disclosure. Both limbs must be satisfied.

disclosureHierarchy.principle: Sections 43C–43H create a HIERARCHY of disclosure recipients. The conditions for protection become progressively MORE STRINGENT as the disclosure moves further from the employer:

tiers.conditions: Lowest threshold — the worker need only make a qualifying disclosure to the employer or a person authorised by the employer to receive such disclosures. No additional conditions beyond s.43B.
Disclosure made in the course of obtaining legal advice — automatically protected regardless of the content test.
Where the worker's employer is appointed by a Minister — disclosure to the relevant Minister is protected.
TWO additional conditions: (1) reasonable belief that the failure falls within the prescribed person's remit; (2) reasonable belief that the information is substantially true.

tiers.recipient: Other person (wider disclosure — media, MP, etc.)
tiers.conditions: MOST stringent — multiple additional conditions including reasonable belief in substantial truth, not for personal gain, reasonable in all circumstances, and one of three preconditions (fear of detriment, no prescribed person, or previous disclosure already made).
Disclosure about an exceptionally serious failure — reasonable in all circumstances, not for personal gain, reasonable belief in substantial truth.

versions.text: In this Act a "protected disclosure" means a qualifying disclosure (as defined by section 43B) which is made by a worker in accordance with any of sections 43C to 43H.
versions.amendedBy: Inserted by Public Interest Disclosure Act 1998 s.1, in force 2 July 1999

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 adds s.43B(1)(da) — sexual harassment as a qualifying disclosure — from 6 April 2026. This expands the s.43B categories but does not amend s.43A itself. The Government has also announced a review of the whistleblowing framework (following the July 2025 Whistleblowing Law Review) — further reforms to the disclosure hierarchy may be expected.
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: LOW for s.43A directly — the gateway definition is unchanged. The expansion of s.43B categories (sexual harassment) broadens the scope of what constitutes a qualifying disclosure.

leadingCases.name: Babula v Waltham Forest College
leadingCases.principle: A disclosure can be a qualifying disclosure under s.43B even if the worker's belief about the wrongdoing turns out to be WRONG — provided the belief was reasonable at the time. The s.43A/43B test is whether the worker REASONABLY BELIEVED the information tended to show wrongdoing, not whether wrongdoing actually occurred.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the worker need not prove the wrongdoing actually occurred. The test is whether the worker had a reasonable belief. A mistaken but reasonable belief is sufficient for a qualifying disclosure under s.43B, and therefore for a protected disclosure under s.43A.
leadingCases.significance: Establishes that the s.43A/43B test is SUBJECTIVE (reasonable belief) not OBJECTIVE (actual wrongdoing). Critical because it means employers cannot defeat a whistleblowing claim simply by proving the worker was wrong about the facts.

leadingCases.name: Chesterton Global Ltd v Nurmohamed
leadingCases.principle: The 'public interest' requirement in s.43B (added by ERRA 2013) does not require the disclosure to be of interest to the public at large. A disclosure about a matter affecting a group of workers (not just the individual) can satisfy the public interest test. The tribunal should consider: the numbers affected, the nature of the interest, the nature of the wrongdoing, and the identity of the wrongdoer.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that a disclosure about alleged overstating of costs affecting 100 managers' commission was in the public interest — it affected a sufficient number of people beyond the individual claimant.
leadingCases.significance: THE foundational EWCA authority on the s.43B public interest test. Cross-referenced from s.43A because the public interest requirement is a component of the qualifying disclosure that feeds into the protected disclosure definition.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Whistleblowing for employees
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance on how to make a protected disclosure.

commonErrors: Not recognising that s.43A requires BOTH a qualifying disclosure AND an appropriate method of disclosure — a worker who discloses information meeting the s.43B test but sends it to an inappropriate recipient (e.g., social media, without meeting the s.43G conditions) does NOT have a protected disclosure. Conversely, a worker who discloses to the right person but whose disclosure does not meet the s.43B test (e.g., not in the public interest) does NOT have a protected disclosure. BOTH limbs must be satisfied

Not understanding the disclosure hierarchy — ss.43C–43H create a progressive hierarchy. Disclosure to the employer (s.43C) has the lowest threshold. Disclosure to a prescribed person (s.43F) has TWO additional conditions. Wider disclosure (s.43G) has the MOST stringent conditions. Workers are incentivised to disclose internally first

Assuming the worker must prove the wrongdoing actually occurred — the s.43B test (which feeds into s.43A) requires only REASONABLE BELIEF that the information tends to show wrongdoing. A worker whose belief turns out to be wrong is still protected if the belief was reasonable at the time (Babula v Waltham Forest [2007] EWCA)

Not knowing that 'public interest' does not mean 'of interest to the public' — the public interest requirement (added by ERRA 2013) is satisfied where the disclosure affects people beyond the individual worker. A group of employees affected by the same wrongdoing is sufficient (Chesterton v Nurmohamed [2018] EWCA). The disclosure does not need to affect the general public

Conflating 'qualifying disclosure' with 'protected disclosure' — a qualifying disclosure (s.43B) is the CONTENT test. A protected disclosure (s.43A) is the COMBINED test of content + method. Using the terms interchangeably creates confusion. Eileen must be precise: qualifying disclosure = what was disclosed; protected disclosure = what was disclosed + to whom

Not recognising that legal advice disclosures are automatically protected — s.43D provides that a disclosure made to a legal adviser in the course of obtaining legal advice is automatically protected. No additional conditions apply. This ensures workers can seek legal advice about potential whistleblowing without fear of losing protection

Not understanding the ERRA 2013 changes — the Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 made three key changes: (1) added the 'public interest' requirement to s.43B; (2) removed the 'good faith' requirement from ss.43C–43H (replaced with up to 25% compensation reduction for lack of good faith); (3) introduced co-worker liability under s.47B(1A). All three affect the s.43A analysis

Treating all external disclosures the same — the conditions for protection vary significantly between s.43F (prescribed person — moderate conditions) and s.43G (wider disclosure — stringent conditions). A worker who discloses to the HSE under s.43F faces lower hurdles than a worker who goes to the press under s.43G. The choice of recipient determines the applicable test

Not recognising that s.43A applies to WORKERS not just employees — the definition of 'worker' for whistleblowing purposes is extended by s.43K to include agency workers, NHS practitioners, police officers, and others. All workers (not just employees) can make protected disclosures

Not advising workers to disclose internally first — while there is no REQUIREMENT to disclose internally before going external, the disclosure hierarchy in ss.43C–43H creates practical incentives to do so. Internal disclosure (s.43C) has the lowest threshold. External disclosure (s.43F/43G) has additional conditions. Workers who disclose internally first are in a stronger position

Not recognising the ERA 2025 expansion — from 6 April 2026, sexual harassment (s.43B(1)(da)) is an explicit qualifying disclosure category. A worker who discloses information about sexual harassment and makes the disclosure in accordance with ss.43C–43H has a protected disclosure under s.43A with full s.47B/s.103A protections

sections.title: Disclosure to prescribed person
sections.summary: A qualifying disclosure is a protected disclosure if made to a person prescribed by the Secretary of State ('prescribed person') AND the worker reasonably believes: (1) the relevant failure falls within any description of matters in respect of which that person is prescribed; AND (2) the information disclosed, and any allegation contained in it, are SUBSTANTIALLY TRUE. This is the 'regulator' route — the second tier in the disclosure hierarchy after disclosure to the employer (s.43C). The prescribed persons are listed in the Public Interest Disclosure (Prescribed Persons) Order 2014 (SI 2014/2418), which contains 80+ bodies including HSE, CQC, FCA, PRA, Ofsted, ICO, Environment Agency, HMRC, Ofcom, Charity Commission, and local authority environmental health. The Order is amended regularly — most recently by SI 2025/604 (sanctions-related functions). Prescribed persons must publish annual reports on disclosures received (s.43FA).
sections.text:
(1) A qualifying disclosure is made in accordance with this section if the worker—

(a) makes the disclosure to a person prescribed by an order made by the Secretary of State for the purposes of this section, and

(b) reasonably believes—

(i) that the relevant failure falls within any description of matters in respect of which that person is so prescribed, and

(ii) that the information disclosed, and any allegation contained in it, are substantially true.
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 43F provides the SECOND-TIER disclosure route — to a regulator or prescribed body. It is more protective than wider disclosure (s.43G) but carries TWO additional conditions beyond the s.43B qualifying disclosure test: (1) the worker must reasonably believe the failure falls within the prescribed person's REMIT (you must go to the RIGHT regulator); (2) the worker must reasonably believe the information is SUBSTANTIALLY TRUE (a higher threshold than the s.43B 'tends to show' test). The 'good faith' requirement was removed by ERRA 2013 — but if the disclosure is not made in good faith, the tribunal may reduce compensation by up to 25%.

condition1_remit.test: The worker reasonably believes the relevant failure falls within any description of matters in respect of which the prescribed person is prescribed
condition1_remit.practicalNote: The worker must go to the RIGHT regulator. A disclosure about health and safety failures should be made to the HSE, not to the FCA. A disclosure about financial misconduct should be made to the FCA, not to the CQC. However, the test is REASONABLE BELIEF — the worker does not need to be correct about the prescribed person's exact remit, only to have a reasonable belief about it.

condition2_substantiallyTrue.test: The worker reasonably believes the information disclosed, and any allegation contained in it, are SUBSTANTIALLY TRUE
condition2_substantiallyTrue.contrast: This is a HIGHER threshold than the s.43B test (which only requires 'tends to show'). Under s.43B, the worker needs to reasonably believe the information tends to show wrongdoing. Under s.43F, the worker must reasonably believe the information is substantially true. The difference is between 'suggesting wrongdoing' and 'believing the facts are accurate'.
condition2_substantiallyTrue.practicalNote: The 'substantially true' test does not require the worker to prove the truth of every detail — only to have a reasonable belief that the substance of the disclosure is true. Minor inaccuracies do not defeat the protection.

prescribedPersons.governingInstrument: Public Interest Disclosure (Prescribed Persons) Order 2014 (SI 2014/2418), as amended
prescribedPersons.latestAmendment: Public Interest Disclosure (Prescribed Persons) (Amendment) Order 2025 (SI 2025/604) — added sanctions-related functions for Secretary of State for Business and Trade, Secretary of State for Transport, and the Treasury

keyPrescribedPersons.matters: Health and social care services in England
Data protection and freedom of information
Environmental health, food safety, health and safety (as enforcing authority)
Serious or complex fraud, bribery, corruption

prescribedPersons.annualReporting: s.43FA (inserted by Small Business, Enterprise and Employment Act 2015) requires prescribed persons to produce ANNUAL REPORTS on disclosures received. The reports must cover the number of disclosures, the types of concerns raised, and actions taken — without identifying individual whistleblowers.

goodFaithRemoval.change: ERRA 2013 s.18(1)(c) removed the requirement that the disclosure be made 'in good faith' from s.43F(1)(a). Before 25 June 2013, the worker had to make the disclosure in good faith to be protected under s.43F. This is no longer required.
goodFaithRemoval.compensationReduction: ERA 1996 s.49(6A) provides that if the tribunal finds the disclosure was not made in good faith, it may reduce compensation by up to 25%. This applies to all disclosure routes, not just s.43F.

versions.text: [s.43F was inserted by PIDA 1998. 'Good faith' requirement removed by ERRA 2013 s.18(1)(c), in force 25 June 2013. The substance of the provision is otherwise unchanged.]
versions.amendedBy: Inserted by PIDA 1998 s.1; amended by ERRA 2013 s.18(1)(c) (removed 'good faith'); prescribed persons updated regularly by SI

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 does not directly amend s.43F. However, the Government published the outcome of the Whistleblowing Law Review in July 2025 and has announced a further consultation on reform of the whistleblowing framework expected in 2026. Potential reforms include: expanding the list of prescribed persons, strengthening prescribed person obligations, creating an independent whistleblowing authority (Office of the Whistleblower), and revising the disclosure hierarchy.
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: MODERATE — any expansion of the prescribed persons list or reform of the disclosure hierarchy will affect the s.43F route.
