leadingCases.name: Korashi v Abertawe Bro Morgannwg University Local Health Board
leadingCases.principle: A disclosure to a prescribed person under s.43F must relate to a matter within the prescribed person's DESCRIPTION of prescribed matters. The worker must direct the disclosure to the body that is prescribed for the TYPE of failure being disclosed. A disclosure about healthcare failures to the HSE (prescribed for health and safety, not healthcare regulation) may not satisfy s.43F(1)(b)(i).
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT examined the requirement that the worker reasonably believes the failure falls within the prescribed person's remit. The prescribed person must be prescribed for the TYPE of matter being disclosed.
leadingCases.significance: Emphasises the importance of disclosing to the RIGHT prescribed person. Workers should check the Prescribed Persons Order to identify which body is prescribed for the type of failure they are disclosing.

leadingCases.name: Darnton v University of Surrey
leadingCases.principle: The 'substantially true' test in s.43F(1)(b)(ii) requires the worker to have a reasonable belief that the information is substantially true — not merely a suspicion or concern. However, the worker does not need to have proof — reasonable belief based on available information is sufficient.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT confirmed that the 'substantially true' threshold requires more than a mere suspicion but less than proof. The worker must have a reasonable basis for believing the substance of the information is true.
leadingCases.significance: Clarifies the 'substantially true' standard — higher than s.43B's 'tends to show' but lower than proof. The worker needs a reasonable basis for the belief, not evidence that would satisfy a court.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Blowing the whistle: list of prescribed people and bodies
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: The official list of prescribed persons under s.43F — workers should check this list before making an external disclosure to a regulator.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Guidance for prescribed persons
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Guidance for prescribed persons on their obligations when receiving disclosures, including annual reporting under s.43FA.

commonErrors: Not knowing that the worker must disclose to the RIGHT prescribed person — s.43F(1)(b)(i) requires the worker to reasonably believe the failure falls within the prescribed person's remit. Disclosing health and safety concerns to the FCA or financial concerns to the CQC may not satisfy s.43F. The worker should check the Prescribed Persons Order (Korashi v Abertawe [2012])

Confusing the s.43F 'substantially true' test with the s.43B 'tends to show' test — s.43F(1)(b)(ii) requires the worker to reasonably believe the information is SUBSTANTIALLY TRUE. This is HIGHER than the s.43B threshold of 'tends to show' wrongdoing. Under s.43B, a suspicion supported by some information may suffice. Under s.43F, the worker must believe the substance is true (Darnton v University of Surrey [2003])

Assuming that 'good faith' is still required for s.43F disclosures — ERRA 2013 removed the good faith requirement from s.43F(1)(a). A disclosure motivated by personal grievance or malice can still be protected under s.43F — but the tribunal may reduce compensation by up to 25% if the disclosure was not in good faith (s.49(6A))

Not checking the Prescribed Persons Order before advising workers — the Order (SI 2014/2418) is amended regularly. New prescribed persons are added, existing descriptions are updated, and bodies are renamed. The latest amendment (SI 2025/604) added sanctions-related functions. Advisers who rely on outdated lists may direct workers to the wrong body

Not recognising that the prescribed person route is the SECOND TIER in the disclosure hierarchy — s.43F sits between s.43C (employer — lowest threshold) and s.43G (wider disclosure — highest threshold). Workers who disclose to a prescribed person without first disclosing to the employer are not penalised — but the s.43F conditions are more stringent than s.43C

Assuming that a disclosure to a prescribed person is automatically protected — it is NOT. The disclosure must meet THREE cumulative tests: (1) s.43B qualifying disclosure test; (2) s.43F(1)(b)(i) — within the prescribed person's remit; (3) s.43F(1)(b)(ii) — substantially true. Failing any one of these defeats the s.43F protection

Not recognising that prescribed persons must publish annual reports — s.43FA requires prescribed persons to publish annual reports on disclosures received. These reports cover the number of disclosures, types of concerns, and actions taken. This creates transparency and accountability in the prescribed person regime

Not considering s.43F when drafting internal whistleblowing policies — internal policies should inform workers about the prescribed person route as an alternative to internal disclosure. Workers who are not aware of the s.43F option may go directly to wider disclosure (s.43G) — which has higher thresholds. Good policies signpost both internal and s.43F routes

Not recognising that the Prescribed Persons Order contains 80+ bodies — the list is extensive and sector-specific. It includes not just major regulators (HSE, CQC, FCA) but also specialist bodies (Certification Officer for trade unions, Pensions Regulator, Gangmasters Licensing Authority, Food Standards Agency, etc.). The full list is published on GOV.UK

Treating the prescribed person as a proxy for the employer — a disclosure to a prescribed person is an EXTERNAL disclosure. The prescribed person is not an extension of the employer. The employer has no right to prevent a worker from disclosing to a prescribed person, and any term purporting to do so is void under s.43J

Not recognising that ERA 2025 may reform the prescribed person framework — the Government's Whistleblowing Law Review (July 2025) examined the adequacy of the current framework. Further consultation on reforms — potentially including an Office of the Whistleblower and expanded prescribed person obligations — is expected in 2026. The landscape may change

sections.title: Note about disciplinary procedures and pensions
sections.summary: The statement of employment particulars given under s.1 must include a note: (a) specifying any disciplinary rules applicable to the worker, or referring the worker to a reasonably accessible document that specifies them; (aa) specifying the disciplinary procedure applicable to disciplinary decisions or decisions to dismiss, or referring the worker to a reasonably accessible document; (b) specifying (i) the person to whom the worker can apply if dissatisfied with any disciplinary decision or decision to dismiss, and (ii) the person to whom the worker can apply to seek redress of any grievance relating to employment, and the manner of application; (c) explaining any further steps following such application. Health and safety rules and procedures are EXCLUDED from s.3 (s.3(2)). Extended to WORKERS (not just employees) from 6 April 2020 by SI 2019/731.
sections.text:
(1) The statement given to a worker under section 1 shall include a note—

(a) specifying any disciplinary rules applicable to the worker or referring the worker to the provisions of a document specifying such rules which is reasonably accessible to the worker,

(aa) specifying any procedure applicable to the taking of disciplinary decisions relating to the worker, or to a decision to dismiss the worker, or referring the worker to the provisions of a document specifying such a procedure which is reasonably accessible to the worker,

(b) specifying—

(i) a person to whom the worker can apply if dissatisfied with any disciplinary decision relating to him or any decision to dismiss him, and

(ii) a person to whom the worker can apply for the purpose of seeking redress of any grievance relating to his employment, and the manner in which any such application should be made, and

(c) where there are further steps consequent on any such application, explaining those steps or referring to the provisions of a document explaining them which is reasonably accessible to the worker.

(2) Subsection (1) does not apply to rules, disciplinary decisions, decisions to dismiss, grievances or procedures relating to health or safety at work.
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 3 ensures that every worker knows: (1) what the employer's disciplinary rules are; (2) what the disciplinary procedure is; (3) who to appeal a disciplinary or dismissal decision to; (4) who to raise a grievance with and how; and (5) what further steps follow. The information can be provided directly in the s.1 statement or by reference to a REASONABLY ACCESSIBLE document (typically a staff handbook, disciplinary policy, or grievance policy). The employer does not need to include the full text of the disciplinary and grievance procedures in the s.1 statement — but must ensure the referenced documents are genuinely accessible to the worker.

accessibilityRequirement.principle: The document containing the disciplinary rules, procedure, or grievance process must be 'reasonably accessible' to the worker. This means the worker must be able to find and read the document without difficulty. A policy on the employer's intranet is accessible if the worker has intranet access. A policy in a locked filing cabinet in the HR director's office is not reasonably accessible.
accessibilityRequirement.practicalNote: Best practice is to provide the full disciplinary and grievance procedure either in the contract of employment, in a staff handbook given to the worker on day one, or on an intranet/shared drive that the worker can access.

healthAndSafetyExclusion.effect: Rules, disciplinary decisions, decisions to dismiss, grievances, and procedures relating to HEALTH OR SAFETY AT WORK are excluded from the s.3 obligation. This means the employer does not need to include H&S disciplinary rules or H&S grievance procedures in the s.1/s.3 statement. H&S matters are governed by separate legislation (Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974, Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999).

acasCodeInteraction.principle: The ACAS Code of Practice on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures is not part of s.3 — but it is the framework against which the fairness of disciplinary and grievance procedures is assessed. An employer whose disciplinary procedure fails to comply with the ACAS Code risks: (1) a finding of unfair dismissal under s.98(4); (2) an uplift of up to 25% on the compensatory award (s.207A TULRCA). The s.3 note should reference a procedure that is consistent with the ACAS Code.
acasCodeInteraction.crossReference: See s.98 (fairness of dismissal — tribunal must consider whether the employer followed a fair procedure, including the ACAS Code).

versions.text: [s.3 was amended by SI 2019/731 to extend the obligation to WORKERS (not just employees) and to require specification of the disciplinary procedure (s.3(1)(aa)) from 6 April 2020. The original provision only required specification of disciplinary rules (s.3(1)(a)).]
versions.amendedBy: ERA 1996 as enacted; amended by EA 2002 s.35 (added s.3(1)(aa)); extended to workers by SI 2019/731 (6 April 2020)

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 does not directly amend s.3. However, the reduction of the unfair dismissal qualifying period to 6 months makes disciplinary and grievance procedures even more critical — employers will face unfair dismissal exposure earlier, and the quality of their disciplinary procedure (referenced in the s.3 note) will be scrutinised from the 6-month mark.
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: MODERATE — the s.3 note becomes more operationally important when the unfair dismissal qualifying period drops to 6 months.

refs: ERA 1996 s.3 (this section — note about disciplinary and grievance procedures)
ERA 1996 s.1 (statement of employment particulars — s.3 is part of the s.1 statement)
ERA 1996 s.4 (statement of changes — changes to s.3 information must be notified)
ERA 1996 s.11 (complaint to tribunal for failure to provide s.1/s.3 statement)
EA 2002 s.38 (penalty for failure to provide complete s.1/s.3 statement — 2 or 4 weeks' pay)
ACAS Code of Practice on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures (the framework for fair procedures)

leadingCases.name: Kulkarni v Milton Keynes Hospital NHS Foundation Trust
leadingCases.principle: Where the employer's disciplinary procedure is set out in a document referred to in the s.1/s.3 statement, the terms of that procedure may be incorporated into the contract of employment. This means the employer must follow the procedure or face a breach of contract claim — in addition to unfair dismissal.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that detailed disciplinary procedures referenced in the employment contract (as required by s.3) can become contractual terms. The employer's failure to follow its own procedure amounted to a breach of contract.
leadingCases.significance: Demonstrates the legal consequences of the s.3 cross-reference — procedures referenced in the s.1 statement may become contractually binding. Employers should consider whether their disciplinary procedures are intended to be contractual or non-contractual.

leadingCases.name: Hussain v Acorn Independent College Ltd
leadingCases.principle: The EA 2002 s.38 penalty (2 or 4 weeks' pay) for failure to provide a complete s.1/s.3 statement is AUTOMATIC where the employee succeeds in another claim and the s.1/s.3 statement was incomplete or absent at the time the proceedings were begun. The tribunal must make the award unless there are exceptional circumstances.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT confirmed that the s.38 penalty is mandatory where the employee succeeds in a claim listed in Schedule 5 to the EA 2002 and the s.1/s.3 statement was incomplete. The tribunal has discretion only on whether to award 2 or 4 weeks' pay.
leadingCases.significance: Confirms that the s.38 penalty is a near-automatic add-on to any successful claim where the s.1/s.3 statement was deficient. Missing disciplinary/grievance information in the s.3 note triggers the penalty.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Code of Practice on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: The ACAS Code is the benchmark for fair disciplinary and grievance procedures. The s.3 note should reference a procedure consistent with the Code.

commonErrors: Not including disciplinary and grievance information in the s.1 statement at all — s.3 requires the s.1 statement to include a note specifying disciplinary rules, the disciplinary procedure, appeal contacts, grievance contacts, and further steps. Omitting this information means the s.1 statement is INCOMPLETE — triggering the EA 2002 s.38 penalty (2 or 4 weeks' pay) if the worker succeeds in any claim listed in Schedule 5

Not making the referenced documents 'reasonably accessible' — the employer can refer the worker to a separate document instead of including the full details in the s.1 statement. But that document must be REASONABLY ACCESSIBLE. A policy that exists but cannot be found, or is only available in a location the worker cannot easily access, does not satisfy s.3

Not recognising that s.3 extends to WORKERS (not just employees) from 6 April 2020 — SI 2019/731 extended the s.1/s.3 obligation to workers. All workers (including those who are not employees) must receive the disciplinary and grievance note. This catches agency workers, casual workers, and others within the s.230(3) definition

Treating the disciplinary procedure as non-contractual when it has been referenced in the s.1 statement — where the s.1/s.3 statement refers to a disciplinary procedure, the terms of that procedure may be incorporated into the contract (Kulkarni v Milton Keynes [2009] EWCA). The employer must then follow the procedure or face a breach of contract claim

Not specifying the person to whom the worker can appeal a disciplinary or dismissal decision — s.3(1)(b)(i) requires the employer to identify the person to whom the worker can apply if dissatisfied with a disciplinary decision or decision to dismiss. Simply stating 'you can appeal' without naming a person or role is insufficient

Not specifying the person to whom the worker can raise a grievance — s.3(1)(b)(ii) requires the employer to identify the person to whom the worker can apply to seek redress of a grievance AND the manner in which the application should be made (e.g., in writing to the line manager, or via a specific email address)

Not recognising the H&S exclusion — s.3(2) excludes rules, decisions, and procedures relating to health or safety at work from the s.3 obligation. H&S matters are governed by separate legislation. The employer does not need to include H&S disciplinary rules in the s.3 note

Not aligning the referenced procedure with the ACAS Code — while the ACAS Code is not legally binding, tribunals must take it into account when assessing the fairness of dismissal under s.98(4). A disciplinary procedure referenced in the s.3 note that does not comply with the ACAS Code creates unfair dismissal exposure and potential 25% uplift on the compensatory award

sections.title: Statement of changes
sections.summary: Where there is a change in any of the particulars required by s.1 or s.3, the employer must give the worker a written statement containing particulars of the change at the earliest opportunity and in any event not later than ONE MONTH after the change. The statement may refer to a reasonably accessible document for certain particulars. This is a continuing obligation — every time the s.1/s.3 information changes, the employer must notify the worker within one month. Failure to provide a s.4 statement is treated the same as failure to provide the s.1 statement — the EA 2002 s.38 penalty applies.
sections.text:
(1) If, after the material date, there is a change in any of the matters particulars of which are required by sections 1 and 3 to be included or referred to in a statement under section 1, the employer shall give to the worker a written statement containing particulars of the change.

(3) A statement under subsection (1) shall be given at the earliest opportunity and, in any event, not later than—

(a) one month after the change in question, or

(b) where that change results from the worker being required to work outside the United Kingdom for a period of more than one month, the time when he leaves the United Kingdom in order to begin so to work, if that is earlier.

(6) A statement under subsection (1) may refer the worker to the provisions of some other document which is reasonably accessible to the worker for a change in any of the matters specified in sections 1(4)(d)(ii) and (iii), (e) and (j) and 3.
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 4 creates a CONTINUING obligation. The s.1 statement is not a one-off document — it must be kept up to date. Whenever any of the required particulars change, the employer must notify the worker in writing within one month. This covers changes to: pay, hours, holiday, sick pay, pension, notice period, job title, place of work, disciplinary/grievance procedures, and all other s.1/s.3 matters. The obligation is triggered by any CHANGE — not just changes the worker might consider significant.

whatTriggerS4.anyChange: Any change to ANY of the particulars required by s.1 or s.3, including: pay rate or structure, working hours, holiday entitlement, sick pay arrangements, pension, notice period, job title or description, place of work, disciplinary or grievance procedures, appeal contacts, collective agreements
whatTriggerS4.excludes: Changes that are already provided for in the contract (e.g., annual pay reviews where the contract states the salary will be reviewed) do not necessarily require a s.4 statement — but best practice is to confirm the new rate in writing. Changes to legislation (e.g., new minimum wage rates) do require notification if they affect the worker's terms.

timingObligation.general: At the earliest opportunity and in any event not later than ONE MONTH after the change (s.4(3)(a))
timingObligation.overseasWork: Where the change results from the worker being required to work outside the UK for more than one month, the statement must be given BEFORE the worker leaves the UK, if that is earlier than one month after the change (s.4(3)(b))

ea2002Penalty.effect: Where a worker succeeds in a claim listed in EA 2002 Sch.5 (unfair dismissal, wages deduction, discrimination, etc.) and the employer has not provided a s.4 statement (or has provided an incomplete or inaccurate one), the tribunal must award 2 weeks' pay (or 4 weeks if it considers it just and equitable). This is the SAME penalty as for failure to provide the s.1 statement. The s.4 obligation is treated as part of the s.1 compliance framework.

versions.text: [s.4 was extended to WORKERS from 6 April 2020 by SI 2019/731. The one-month notification period is unchanged since enactment.]
versions.amendedBy: ERA 1996 as enacted; extended to workers by SI 2019/731 (6 April 2020)

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 does not directly amend s.4. However, the expanded scope of the s.1 statement (which now includes additional day-one information from April 2020) means the s.4 continuing obligation covers a wider range of particulars than before.
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: LOW — the provision is unchanged, but the wider scope of s.1 means more changes trigger the s.4 obligation.

refs: ERA 1996 s.4 (this section — statement of changes)
ERA 1996 s.1 (statement of employment particulars — the original statement that s.4 updates)
ERA 1996 s.3 (disciplinary and grievance note — changes to s.3 information also trigger s.4)
ERA 1996 s.11 (complaint to tribunal for failure to provide s.1/s.4 statement)
EA 2002 s.38 (penalty for failure to provide or update — 2 or 4 weeks' pay)

leadingCases.name: Eagland v British Telecommunications plc
leadingCases.principle: The s.4 obligation to notify changes applies even where the change was made by agreement. The employer cannot assume the worker is aware of the change simply because the worker agreed to it — the written notification must still be provided within one month.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the employer's obligation to provide a written statement of changes arises whenever the particulars change, regardless of whether the change was agreed or imposed. The worker is entitled to written confirmation.
leadingCases.significance: Confirms that s.4 is a DOCUMENTATION obligation, not just an information obligation. Even agreed changes must be confirmed in writing within one month.

leadingCases.name: Cumbria County Council v Dow (No. 1)
leadingCases.principle: Where the employer fails to provide a s.4 statement after a change and the worker subsequently succeeds in a claim, the EA 2002 s.38 penalty is triggered. The tribunal must award 2 weeks' pay (minimum) and may award 4 weeks' pay if just and equitable.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT confirmed that the s.38 penalty applies to failures to provide s.4 statements in the same way as failures to provide s.1 statements. The obligation is continuing — each unremedied failure is potentially actionable.
leadingCases.significance: Confirms the penalty exposure for s.4 failures. Important because many employers provide the initial s.1 statement but fail to update it when changes occur — creating cumulative s.38 exposure.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Employment contracts
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance on the obligation to provide written statements including the s.4 update obligation.

commonErrors: Not providing a s.4 statement at all when terms change — many employers provide the initial s.1 statement but fail to notify changes within one month. Every unremedied failure creates EA 2002 s.38 penalty exposure (2 or 4 weeks' pay) when the worker succeeds in another claim

Assuming that agreed changes do not need to be notified in writing — the s.4 obligation applies even where the change was AGREED between the employer and worker (Eagland v BT [1993] EWCA). The written notification must still be provided within one month. Verbal agreements, emails, and informal conversations do not satisfy s.4

Not meeting the ONE MONTH deadline — s.4(3)(a) requires the statement to be given at the earliest opportunity and in any event within one month. This is a MAXIMUM, not a target. Late notification is a breach even if the worker was aware of the change from another source

Not recognising that the s.4 obligation extends to WORKERS — from 6 April 2020 (SI 2019/731), the s.1/s.3/s.4 obligations apply to workers (not just employees). Changes to a worker's terms must be notified in writing within one month

Not recognising which changes trigger the s.4 obligation — ANY change to ANY s.1 or s.3 particular triggers s.4. This includes: pay changes, hours changes, holiday changes, pension changes, notice period changes, job title changes, location changes, disciplinary procedure changes, grievance procedure changes, and changes to named contacts for appeals/grievances

Assuming the worker will 'just know' about changes — the s.4 obligation is a DOCUMENTATION requirement. Even if the worker is aware of the change (e.g., because they attend a meeting where a new policy is announced), the employer must provide a written statement confirming the change within one month

Not providing a s.4 statement when changing disciplinary or grievance procedures — changes to the disciplinary rules, disciplinary procedure, appeal contacts, grievance contacts, or further steps (all required by s.3) trigger the s.4 obligation. Updating the staff handbook without providing a s.4 statement is insufficient

Not recognising the cumulative EA 2002 s.38 penalty exposure — each failure to provide a s.4 statement is a separate breach. An employer who fails to notify multiple changes over several years accumulates multiple potential s.38 penalties. When the worker succeeds in a claim, ALL outstanding s.1/s.3/s.4 failures can trigger the penalty (Cumbria v Dow [2008])

sections.title: References to employment tribunals
sections.summary: Where an employer does not give a worker a statement as required by s.1 or s.4, or the statement is incomplete or inaccurate, the worker may apply to the employment tribunal for a determination of what particulars OUGHT to have been included. The employer may also apply. The tribunal cannot invent terms — it can only determine what particulars should have been given based on the evidence. The time limit is 3 months from the date the employment ceased (or such further period as the tribunal considers reasonable where it was not reasonably practicable to present within 3 months). From October 2026, the time limit extends to 6 months under ERA 2025. The s.11 reference operates alongside (but separately from) the EA 2002 s.38 penalty — s.11 gets the worker a determination of the correct particulars; s.38 gets the worker a financial penalty (2 or 4 weeks' pay) when they succeed in another listed claim.
sections.text:
(1) Where an employer does not give a worker a statement as required by section 1, 4 or 8 (either because the employer gives the worker no statement or because the statement the employer gives does not comply with what is required), the worker may require a reference to be made to an employment tribunal to determine what particulars ought to have been included or referred to in a statement so as to comply with the requirements of the section concerned.

(3) An employment tribunal shall not consider a reference under this section in a case where the employment to which the reference relates has ceased unless an application requiring the reference to be made was made—

(a) before the end of the period of three months beginning with the date on which the employment ceased, or

(b) within such further period as the tribunal considers reasonable in a case where it is satisfied that it was not reasonably practicable for the application to be made before the end of that period of three months.
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 11 is the ENFORCEMENT mechanism for the s.1/s.3/s.4 statement obligations. It allows the worker to refer the matter to the tribunal to DETERMINE what should have been included. The tribunal's role is to identify what the correct particulars are — based on the evidence of what was agreed, what applied, and what the statutory requirements demand. The tribunal CANNOT invent terms that were never agreed — it can only fill in the blanks where the employer failed to provide information that should have been given. The s.11 reference is most useful during employment (to force the employer to comply) and after employment (to establish what the terms were for the purpose of other claims).

interactionWithS38.principle: s.11 and EA 2002 s.38 serve DIFFERENT purposes. s.11 = determination of correct particulars (declaratory). s.38 = financial penalty (2 or 4 weeks' pay) when the worker succeeds in another listed claim and the s.1 statement was incomplete or absent. The worker can pursue both — s.11 to establish the correct terms, and s.38 for the penalty when they win another claim.
interactionWithS38.practicalNote: In practice, standalone s.11 references are rare — most workers raise the s.1 statement failure as part of a broader claim (unfair dismissal, wages deduction, discrimination) and rely on s.38 for the penalty. The s.11 reference is most valuable where the correct terms are genuinely in dispute.

timeLimit.current: 3 months from the date employment ceased (s.11(3)(a)), or such further period as reasonable if not reasonably practicable within 3 months (s.11(3)(b)). ACAS EC pauses the clock.
timeLimit.era2025: From October 2026, the primary time limit extends to 6 months under ERA 2025.

versions.amendedBy: ERA 1996 as enacted; extended to workers by SI 2019/731 (6 April 2020)

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 extends the time limit from 3 months to 6 months (from October 2026). This applies to s.11 references alongside all other tribunal time limits.
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: LOW — the time limit extension gives workers more time to bring s.11 references.

refs: ERA 1996 s.11 (this section — tribunal reference for particulars)
ERA 1996 s.1 (statement of employment particulars — the obligation enforced by s.11)
ERA 1996 s.3 (disciplinary/grievance note — also enforced by s.11)
ERA 1996 s.4 (statement of changes — also enforced by s.11)
ERA 1996 s.12 (tribunal determination — what the tribunal can order)
EA 2002 s.38 (financial penalty — operates alongside s.11)

leadingCases.name: Mears v Safecar Security Ltd
leadingCases.principle: The tribunal's role under s.11 is to DETERMINE what particulars should have been given — not to INVENT contractual terms. The tribunal can identify what was agreed (expressly or impliedly) but cannot impose terms that neither party agreed to.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the tribunal's power under s.11 is declaratory — it determines what the correct particulars are based on the evidence. It cannot create contractual terms that did not exist.
leadingCases.significance: THE foundational authority on the scope of the s.11 power. The tribunal determines — it does not create.

leadingCases.name: Southern Cross Healthcare Co Ltd v Perkins
leadingCases.principle: The s.11 reference can be used even after employment has ended, provided the application is made within the time limit. The determination is useful for establishing what the terms were for the purpose of other claims (e.g., unfair dismissal, wrongful dismissal, wages deduction).
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal confirmed that s.11 references can be made after employment ends and that the determination can inform other claims.
leadingCases.significance: Confirms the post-employment utility of s.11 — particularly useful where disputed terms affect the outcome of other claims.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Employment contracts
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance on the obligation to provide written statements and the enforcement mechanism.

commonErrors: Not knowing that s.11 exists — many employers and workers are unaware that the tribunal can determine what particulars should have been given. The s.11 reference is under-used relative to the s.38 penalty, but it can be valuable where the correct terms are genuinely disputed

Confusing s.11 (determination of correct particulars) with EA 2002 s.38 (financial penalty) — they serve DIFFERENT purposes and operate independently. s.11 = 'what should the statement have said?' s.38 = '2 or 4 weeks' pay penalty because the statement was missing/incomplete when you brought your other claim'

Thinking the tribunal can create new contractual terms under s.11 — the tribunal can only DETERMINE what the correct particulars are based on the evidence. It cannot impose terms that were never agreed (Mears v Safecar [1983] EWCA). If there is no agreement on a particular point, the tribunal may be unable to determine it

Not applying within the time limit — the application must be made within 3 months of the date employment ceased (extending to 6 months from October 2026). ACAS Early Conciliation pauses the clock. Late applications are only accepted where it was not reasonably practicable to apply in time

Not recognising that s.11 applies to WORKERS from 6 April 2020 — the s.1/s.3/s.4/s.11 framework was extended to workers by SI 2019/731. Workers (not just employees) can apply for a tribunal determination of their correct particulars

Not using s.11 alongside other claims — the most effective use of s.11 is as part of a broader claim. The determination of correct particulars can support an unfair dismissal claim (what were the correct notice terms?), a wages deduction claim (what was the correct pay?), or a wrongful dismissal claim (what were the contractual terms?)

Assuming the s.38 penalty replaces the need for s.11 — the s.38 penalty is a financial award. It does not determine what the correct terms are. Where the terms are disputed, the s.11 reference is the mechanism for establishing the facts — the s.38 penalty is the consequence of the failure
Not recognising that ERA 2025 extends the time limit to 6 months — from October 2026, the s.11 time limit will extend from 3 months to 6 months, in line with all other primary tribunal time limits

sections.title: Excepted deductions
sections.summary: Section 14 sets out the EXCEPTIONS to the s.13 prohibition on unauthorised deductions from wages. Six categories of deduction are permitted: (1) statutory deductions required by legislation (income tax, NICs, student loan repayments, attachment of earnings orders); (2) deductions authorised by the worker's CONTRACT (provided the worker has been given a copy of the relevant contractual provision or had it explained in writing BEFORE the deduction was made); (3) deductions where the worker has given prior WRITTEN CONSENT; (4) deductions for OVERPAYMENT of wages or expenses; (5) deductions because of the worker taking part in a STRIKE or other industrial action; (6) deductions required by a COURT ORDER. These exceptions are critical — a deduction that falls within s.14 is lawful even without the s.13(1)(a) or (b) authorisation.
sections.text:
(1) Section 13 does not apply to a deduction from a worker's wages made by his employer where the purpose of the deduction is the reimbursement of the employer in respect of—

(a) an overpayment of wages, or

(b) an overpayment in respect of expenses incurred by the worker in carrying out his employment,

made (for any reason) by the employer to the worker.

(2) Section 13 does not apply to a deduction from a worker's wages made by his employer in consequence of any disciplinary proceedings if those proceedings were held by virtue of a statutory provision.

(3) Section 13 does not apply to a deduction from a worker's wages made by his employer in pursuance of a requirement imposed on the employer by a statutory provision to deduct and pay over to a public authority amounts determined by that authority as being due to it from the worker if the deduction is made in accordance with the relevant determination of that authority.

(4) Section 13 does not apply to a deduction from a worker's wages made by his employer in pursuance of any arrangements which have been established—

(a) in accordance with a relevant provision of his contract to the inclusion of which in the contract the worker has signified his agreement or consent in writing, or

(b) otherwise with the prior agreement or consent of the worker signified in writing,

and under which the employer is to deduct and pay over to a third person amounts notified to the employer by that person as being due to him from the worker, if the deduction is made in accordance with the relevant notification by that person.

(5) Section 13 does not apply to a deduction from a worker's wages made by his employer where the worker has taken part in a strike or other industrial action and the deduction is made by the employer on account of the worker's having taken part in that strike or other action.

(6) Section 13 does not apply to a deduction from a worker's wages made by his employer with the prior agreement or consent of the worker signified in writing where the purpose of the deduction is the satisfaction (whether wholly or in part) of an order of a court or tribunal requiring the payment of an amount by the worker to the employer.
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 14 is the SAFE HARBOUR for employers making deductions. If a deduction falls within one of the s.14 categories, it is lawful regardless of whether the s.13(1)(a) or (b) authorisation was given. The most commonly relied-upon exceptions are: overpayment recovery (s.14(1)), statutory deductions (s.14(3) — tax, NICs), and contractual/consent deductions (s.14(4)). The overpayment exception is particularly important — it allows the employer to recover overpaid wages without the worker's prior consent, though the employer must still act reasonably in the manner and timing of recovery.

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 does not amend s.14. The exceptions to the wages deduction prohibition are unchanged.
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: LOW — the exceptions themselves are unchanged.

refs: ERA 1996 s.14 (this section — excepted deductions)
ERA 1996 s.13 (prohibition on unauthorised deductions — the core prohibition that s.14 qualifies)
ERA 1996 s.23 (complaint to tribunal for unlawful deductions)
ERA 1996 s.27 (definition of 'wages')

leadingCases.name: SIP Industrial Products Ltd v Swinn
leadingCases.principle: The overpayment exception in s.14(1) allows the employer to recover overpaid wages without the worker's consent. However, the employer must ensure the overpayment was genuine — if the payment was in fact owed (e.g., correct commission, correct overtime), recovering it is an unlawful deduction under s.13, not an overpayment recovery under s.14(1).
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT confirmed that the s.14(1) overpayment exception applies to genuine overpayments only. If the employer incorrectly characterises a properly owed payment as an overpayment and deducts it, the deduction is unlawful under s.13.
leadingCases.significance: Prevents employers from using the overpayment exception to recover payments they simply wish they hadn't made. The overpayment must be genuine.

leadingCases.name: Sunderland Polytechnic v Evans
leadingCases.principle: The contractual/consent exception in s.14(4) requires the worker to have agreed IN WRITING BEFORE the deduction is made. Retrospective consent does not satisfy s.14(4). The consent must be genuine, informed, and not obtained under duress.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the written consent required by s.14(4) must be given before the deduction — post-deduction ratification is insufficient.
leadingCases.significance: Confirms the temporal requirement — consent BEFORE the deduction. Employers who deduct first and seek agreement afterwards are in breach of s.13.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Understanding your pay — deductions from your pay
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance on what deductions are lawful.

commonErrors: Not knowing the six categories of excepted deduction — s.14 lists six situations where deductions from wages are lawful: (1) overpayment recovery; (2) statutory disciplinary proceedings; (3) statutory deductions (tax, NI, student loans); (4) contractual/consent deductions to third parties; (5) strike deductions; (6) court order satisfaction. A deduction that falls within any of these categories does NOT need s.13(1)(a) or (b) authorisation

Misusing the overpayment exception to recover payments that were properly owed — s.14(1) only covers GENUINE overpayments. If the employer paid correct commission, correct overtime, or correct bonuses and then tries to 'recover' them, the deduction is unlawful under s.13 (SIP v Swinn [1994]). The overpayment must be a genuine error

Obtaining written consent AFTER the deduction — s.14(4) requires the worker to have agreed or consented IN WRITING BEFORE the deduction is made. Retrospective consent is insufficient (Sunderland Polytechnic v Evans [1993]). The employer must secure written agreement before deducting

Assuming all contractual deduction clauses are automatically valid — even where the contract authorises a deduction, the worker must have been given a copy of the contractual provision or had it explained in writing BEFORE the deduction. A clause buried in a contract the worker has never seen does not satisfy the requirements

Not recognising that the overpayment exception does not require prior consent — unlike other exceptions, s.14(1) allows the employer to recover overpaid wages WITHOUT the worker's consent. However, the employer should still act reasonably in the manner and timing of recovery — particularly where the overpayment was the employer's error and recovery would cause hardship

Not understanding the statutory deduction exception — s.14(3) covers deductions required by law, including income tax (PAYE), National Insurance contributions, student loan repayments, and attachment of earnings orders. These are not 'deductions' in the s.13 sense — they are statutory obligations that the employer must comply with

Not recognising the strike deduction exception — s.14(5) permits the employer to deduct pay for periods when the worker was on strike or taking industrial action. This exception is absolute — no prior consent is required. However, the deduction must be proportionate to the time not worked

Assuming the s.14 exceptions override all claims — the s.14 exceptions only affect the s.13 prohibition. They do not prevent claims for breach of contract, discrimination, or other causes of action. A deduction that is lawful under s.14 may still be a breach of contract if it violates the employment agreement

sections.title: Complaints to employment tribunals — wages deductions
sections.summary: A worker may present a complaint to an employment tribunal that their employer has made an unauthorised deduction from wages contrary to s.13, or has received an unauthorised payment from the worker contrary to s.15. The time limit is 3 months from the date of the deduction (or last in a series of deductions), or such further period as the tribunal considers reasonable where it was not reasonably practicable to present within 3 months. From October 2026, the primary time limit extends to 6 months under ERA 2025. The 'SERIES OF DEDUCTIONS' concept (s.23(3)) is critically important — where the employer makes a series of deductions (of the same kind and at regular intervals), the time limit runs from the LAST deduction in the series, allowing the worker to claim back all deductions in the series, not just the most recent one.
sections.text:
(1) A worker may present a complaint to an employment tribunal—

(a) that his employer has made a deduction from his wages in contravention of section 13 (including a deduction made in contravention of that section as it applies by virtue of section 18(2)),

(b) that his employer has received from him a payment in contravention of section 15 (including a payment received in contravention of that section as it applies by virtue of section 20(1)),

(c) that his employer has made a deduction from his wages in contravention of section 86 as it applies by virtue of section 90,

or that his employer has received from him a payment in contravention of section 86 as it applies by virtue of section 90.

(2) Subject to subsection (4), an employment tribunal shall not consider a complaint under this section unless it is presented before the end of the period of three months beginning with—

(a) in the case of a complaint relating to a deduction by the employer, the date of payment of the wages from which the deduction was made, or

(b) in the case of a complaint relating to a payment received by the employer, the date when the payment was received.

(3) Where a complaint is brought under this section in respect of—

(a) a series of deductions or payments, or

(b) a number of payments falling within subsection (1)(d) and made in pursuance of demands for payment,

the references in subsection (2) to the deduction or payment are to the last deduction or payment in the series or to the last of the payments so falling.
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 23 is the COMPLAINT mechanism for unlawful wages deductions. Two critical features: (1) the TIME LIMIT runs from the date of the deduction (not from when the worker discovers it); (2) the SERIES OF DEDUCTIONS rule (s.23(3)) allows the worker to claim back ALL deductions in a connected series — the time limit runs from the LAST deduction. This means a worker who has suffered monthly underpayments over several years can claim the entire series if they bring the claim within 3 months of the last underpayment.

seriesOfDeductions.principle: Where the employer makes a SERIES of deductions, the time limit runs from the LAST deduction in the series. This allows the worker to claim all deductions in the series, not just the most recent one. A 'series' requires: deductions of the same kind, made at regular intervals, pursuant to the same defect or failing.
seriesOfDeductions.bearGroupLimit: In Bear Scotland Ltd v Fulton [2015] ICR 221, the EAT held that a gap of MORE THAN THREE MONTHS between consecutive deductions breaks the series. This limits how far back a worker can claim — if there is a gap of more than 3 months between any two deductions in the series, only the deductions after the gap can be claimed.
seriesOfDeductions.era2025Note: When the primary time limit extends to 6 months (October 2026), the gap that breaks a series may also effectively extend — though this has not been tested.

versions.amendedBy: ERA 1996 as enacted; time limit extended from 3 months to 6 months by ERA 2025 (from October 2026)

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 extends the primary time limit from 3 months to 6 months (from October 2026). This affects both the initial claim deadline (6 months from the deduction) and potentially the gap that breaks a 'series of deductions' (Bear Scotland — currently 3 months).
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: MODERATE — the extended time limit gives workers significantly more time to bring wages deduction claims. The interaction with the Bear Scotland series gap rule is untested.

ERA 1996 s.13 (prohibition on unauthorised deductions — the substantive right)
ERA 1996 s.14 (excepted deductions — defences to s.13 claims)
ERA 1996 s.24 (determination of complaints — remedy)
ERA 1996 s.27 (definition of 'wages')

leadingCases.name: Bear Scotland Ltd v Fulton
leadingCases.principle: A gap of MORE THAN THREE MONTHS between consecutive deductions in a series breaks the series for the purposes of s.23(3). Only the deductions after the gap can be claimed. This limits the retrospective reach of wages deduction claims — particularly holiday pay claims where employers have systematically excluded overtime from holiday pay calculations.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that a series of deductions requires a sufficient connection between the deductions. A gap of more than 3 months between any two deductions breaks the chain — the worker can only claim deductions from the most recent unbroken series.
leadingCases.significance: THE foundational authority on the 'series of deductions' time limit. Created the 3-month gap rule that limits retrospective claims. Particularly important for holiday pay claims (where underpayment may have continued for years).

leadingCases.name: Delaney v Staples (t/a De Montfort Recruitment)
leadingCases.principle: The s.23 complaint is for deductions from WAGES — it does not cover payments that are not 'wages' within the s.27 definition. A payment in lieu of notice (PILON) that is not contractually required is not 'wages' and cannot be claimed under s.23. The worker must bring a breach of contract claim instead.
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords held that a non-contractual payment in lieu of notice was not 'wages' within the statutory definition. The worker's claim for the PILON had to be brought as a breach of contract claim, not a wages deduction claim under s.23.
leadingCases.significance: THE foundational UKHL authority on the boundary of the s.23 wages deduction claim. If it's not 'wages' under s.27, it's not claimable under s.23 — the worker must use a different claim route.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Make a claim to an employment tribunal
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance on how to bring an employment tribunal claim including wages deduction claims.

commonErrors: Missing the time limit — the claim must be presented within 3 months of the date of the deduction (or the last deduction in a series) — extending to 6 months from October 2026. The time runs from the date of PAYMENT, not from when the worker discovered the deduction. Late claims are only accepted where it was not reasonably practicable to claim in time

Not understanding the 'series of deductions' rule — s.23(3) allows the worker to claim ALL deductions in a connected series by bringing the claim within the time limit of the LAST deduction. This can extend the claim back over months or years. The series must be connected — deductions of the same kind, at regular intervals, from the same defect

Not knowing the Bear Scotland 3-month gap rule — a gap of more than 3 months between consecutive deductions breaks the series (Bear Scotland v Fulton [2015]). Only the deductions after the gap can be claimed. Employers who correct an underpayment for one month but then resume it may inadvertently break and restart the series

Trying to claim non-wages payments under s.23 — the s.23 complaint only covers deductions from WAGES as defined by s.27. Payments that are not 'wages' (non-contractual PILON, ex gratia payments, loans, discretionary bonuses in some circumstances) cannot be claimed under s.23 (Delaney v Staples [1992] UKHL). The worker must use breach of contract instead

Not understanding that s.23 covers both deductions AND payments — s.23(1)(a) covers deductions FROM wages; s.23(1)(b) covers payments TO the employer in contravention of s.15. Both are claimable. An employer who requires a worker to make a payment (e.g., for uniforms, tools, training costs) in contravention of s.15 faces a s.23 claim

Not recognising that the time limit runs from the date of PAYMENT, not from the date the deduction was decided — s.23(2)(a) specifies that the time runs from the date of payment of the wages FROM WHICH the deduction was made. If the deduction is made from the March payslip paid on 28 March, the time runs from 28 March — not from when the employer decided to make the deduction

Not filing ACAS Early Conciliation before bringing the claim — all s.23 claims are subject to mandatory ACAS Early Conciliation (s.18A Employment Tribunals Act 1996). The worker must contact ACAS before filing the ET1. The ACAS EC period (now 12 weeks from 1 December 2025) pauses the time limit

Not recognising that ERA 2025 extends the time limit to 6 months — from October 2026, the primary s.23 time limit extends from 3 months to 6 months. Combined with the 12-week ACAS EC period, workers will have up to 9-10 months from the deduction to bring a claim. The interaction with the Bear Scotland series gap rule is untested but may effectively extend the gap threshold

parts.title: Part VIII — Maternity and Parental Leave (Supplementary)

sections.title: Maternity leave — ordinary, additional, and protection
sections.summary: Sections 71–75 establish the statutory maternity leave framework. s.71: Ordinary Maternity Leave (OML) — the first 26 weeks. s.73: Additional Maternity Leave (AML) — the following 26 weeks (52 weeks total). s.74: Protection during maternity leave — redundancy priority and dismissal protection. Maternity leave is a DAY-ONE right — no qualifying period (the right to Statutory Maternity PAY has a separate 26-week qualifying period). The detail is in the Maternity and Parental Leave etc. Regulations 1999 (SI 1999/3312). During OML, the employee's terms and conditions (except remuneration) are preserved. During AML, similar protections apply. Dismissal for a reason connected to pregnancy or maternity leave is automatically unfair (s.99). From April 2024, the redundancy priority right (Reg.10) is extended to 18 months after the child's birth. ERA 2025: enhanced dismissal protections for pregnant employees and mothers on maternity leave expected in 2027.
sections.text:
Section 71: (1) An employee may, provided that she satisfies any conditions which may be prescribed, be absent from work at any time during an ordinary maternity leave period.

Section 73: (1) An employee who satisfies prescribed conditions may be absent from work at any time during an additional maternity leave period.

Section 74: (1) Regulations under section 71 or 73 may make provision about redundancy during an ordinary or additional maternity leave period. (3) Regulations may include provision requiring an employer to offer alternative employment and provision for dismissal to be treated as unfair.
sections.keyPrinciple: Sections 71–74 are FRAMEWORK provisions — they empower the Secretary of State to make regulations governing maternity leave. The substantive detail is in the Maternity and Parental Leave etc. Regulations 1999 (SI 1999/3312). The ERA provisions establish the right to OML (26 weeks) and AML (26 weeks) = 52 weeks total. The distinction between OML and AML matters for: terms and conditions (fuller protection during OML), the right to return (to the same job after OML, to the same or suitable alternative after AML), and the calculation of contractual entitlements.

oml.conditions: Day-one right. The employee must notify the employer of the expected week of childbirth and the intended start date. 28 days' notice required (or as soon as reasonably practicable).
oml.termsPreserved: All terms and conditions EXCEPT remuneration are preserved during OML (Reg.9). The employment contract continues throughout.

aml.conditions: Follows automatically from OML — no additional notification required.
aml.termsPreserved: Similar protections to OML apply. The employer's obligations continue.

structure.compulsoryMaternityLeave: s.72 — the employee MUST take at least 2 weeks' compulsory maternity leave from the date of childbirth (4 weeks for factory workers). The employer commits a criminal offence if it permits the employee to work during the compulsory period.
structure.rightToReturn: After OML: right to return to the SAME job on the same terms (Reg.18(1)). After AML: right to return to the same job, or if not reasonably practicable, a suitable and appropriate job on no less favourable terms (Reg.18(2)).

redundancyPriority.provision: Reg.10 of SI 1999/3312 (as amended by the Protection from Redundancy (Pregnancy and Family Leave) Act 2023 and the Maternity Leave, Adoption Leave and Shared Parental Leave (Amendment) Regulations 2024 (SI 2024/475))
redundancyPriority.current: Where an employee on maternity leave (or within 18 months of the child's birth) is made redundant and a suitable alternative vacancy exists, the employer MUST offer it to the employee WITH PRIORITY — before offering it to other employees. Failure to offer the priority alternative is automatically unfair (s.99).
redundancyPriority.extendedPeriod: From 6 April 2024, the Reg.10 priority period was extended from the end of maternity leave to 18 MONTHS after the child's birth. This means the employee has redundancy priority for up to approximately 2 years and 9 months from the start of pregnancy.

era2025EnhancedProtection.effect: ERA 2025 gives the Government power to make dismissal of pregnant employees or those on/returned from maternity leave automatically unfair EXCEPT in specified circumstances. Consultation closed January 2026. Two options under consideration: (1) higher standard of fairness for dismissal (stricter than s.98(4)); (2) narrowing or removing some fair reasons for dismissal (e.g., restricting redundancy dismissal to business closure or financial distress only). The details will be in regulations.
era2025EnhancedProtection.practicalNote: This will create the most protected category of employee in UK law — pregnant women and mothers within the protection period may only be dismissable in the most extreme circumstances.

versions.amendedBy: Part VIII substituted by Employment Relations Act 1999 s.7, Sch.4 Pt.I. SI 1999/3312 provides the detail. Protection from Redundancy (Pregnancy and Family Leave) Act 2023 extended Reg.10.

pendingAmendment.effect: Enhanced dismissal protections for pregnant employees and mothers on/returned from maternity leave — expected 2027. Will make dismissal automatically unfair except in specified circumstances (consultation closed January 2026).
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: VERY HIGH — will create the most protected category of employee in UK law.

ERA 1996 s.72 (compulsory maternity leave — 2 weeks minimum)
ERA 1996 s.99 (dismissal for maternity — automatically unfair)
Maternity and Parental Leave etc. Regulations 1999 (SI 1999/3312) — the substantive detail
Protection from Redundancy (Pregnancy and Family Leave) Act 2023 (extended Reg.10)

leadingCases.name: New Southern Railway Ltd v Quinn
leadingCases.principle: An employee on maternity leave who is made redundant must be offered a suitable alternative vacancy in priority over other employees (Reg.10). The employer cannot require the employee on maternity leave to compete for the vacancy through a selection process.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the Reg.10 priority is ABSOLUTE — the employer must offer the suitable alternative directly, without requiring the employee to apply or compete.
leadingCases.significance: THE key authority on the Reg.10 priority — it is an absolute right, not a right to be considered alongside others.

leadingCases.name: Blundell v Governing Body of St Andrew's Catholic Primary School
leadingCases.principle: The right to return after AML is to the same job, or if not reasonably practicable, a suitable and appropriate job. The employer cannot unilaterally change the employee's role during AML and present the changed role as the 'return' — this may constitute constructive dismissal.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the employer's failure to return the employee to her original role after AML, when it was reasonably practicable to do so, was a breach of Reg.18.
leadingCases.significance: Establishes the practical application of the Reg.18 return right — the employer must return the employee to the same job unless genuinely not reasonably practicable.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Maternity leave and pay
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance on maternity leave entitlements.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Maternity leave and pay
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance on maternity leave rights.

commonErrors: Confusing the right to maternity LEAVE (day-one, no qualifying period) with the right to Statutory Maternity PAY (26 weeks' service by the 15th week before EWC) — the leave right and the pay right have DIFFERENT qualifying periods. An employee with 3 months' service can take 52 weeks' maternity leave but may not qualify for SMP

Not recognising the compulsory maternity leave period — s.72 makes it a CRIMINAL OFFENCE for the employer to permit an employee to work during the 2 weeks immediately following childbirth (4 weeks for factory workers). This applies even if the employee wants to return early

Not offering redundancy priority to employees on maternity leave — Reg.10 (as extended from April 2024) requires the employer to offer suitable alternative vacancies WITH PRIORITY to employees on maternity leave or within 18 months of the child's birth. The priority is ABSOLUTE — no competitive selection (New Southern Railway v Quinn [2006])

Failing to preserve terms and conditions during OML — Reg.9 requires all terms and conditions (except remuneration) to continue during OML. This includes annual leave accrual, pension contributions (on the paid period), car allowance, and contractual benefits. The employee's status and seniority are preserved

Not understanding the right to return — after OML: right to return to the SAME job. After AML: same job, or if not reasonably practicable, a suitable alternative on no less favourable terms (Reg.18). Changing the employee's role without justification is a breach (Blundell v St Andrew's [2007])

Not knowing that ERA 2025 will create enhanced dismissal protections — from 2027, dismissal of pregnant employees or those on/returned from maternity leave will be automatically unfair except in specified circumstances (consultation closed January 2026). This will create the most protected employee category in UK law

Treating maternity leave absences as performance issues — any adverse treatment connected to maternity leave (negative appraisals, missed promotions, exclusion from projects, loss of bonus) is a detriment under s.47C and may constitute pregnancy discrimination under EqA 2010 s.18

Not providing 10 Keeping in Touch (KIT) days — employees on maternity leave may work up to 10 KIT days without ending their leave or losing SMP. These are voluntary — the employer cannot require the employee to work, and the employee cannot insist. KIT days allow the employee to attend training, team meetings, or gradual return preparation

sections.title: Parental leave — entitlement and complaint
sections.summary: s.76: An employee who has been continuously employed for a qualifying period is entitled to be absent from work on PARENTAL LEAVE for the purpose of caring for a child. The entitlement is 18 weeks per child, unpaid, available until the child reaches 18. From 6 APRIL 2026, the qualifying period is REMOVED by ERA 2025 — parental leave becomes a DAY-ONE right (previously 1 year's service required). Leave must be taken in blocks of 1 week (unless the child is disabled), maximum 4 weeks per year per child, with 21 days' notice. s.80: An employee may present a complaint to the tribunal that the employer unreasonably refused or postponed parental leave. The details are in the Maternity and Parental Leave etc. Regulations 1999 (SI 1999/3312).
sections.text:
Section 76: (1) An employee who—
(a) has been continuously employed for a period of not less than one year, and
(b) has, or expects to have, responsibility for a child,
is entitled, in accordance with regulations under section 76, to be absent from work on parental leave for the purpose of caring for that child.

Section 80: (1) An employee may present a complaint to an employment tribunal that his employer—
(a) has unreasonably postponed a period of parental leave requested by the employee, or
(b) has prevented or attempted to prevent the employee from taking parental leave.
sections.keyPrinciple: Parental leave is UNPAID time off to care for a child. 18 weeks per child, available until the child reaches 18. From 6 April 2026, it is a DAY-ONE right (ERA 2025 s.15 removes the 1-year qualifying period). The leave is separate from maternity leave, paternity leave, and shared parental leave. It covers any parent with 'responsibility for a child' (biological parents, adoptive parents, and anyone with parental responsibility under the Children Act 1989). The employer can POSTPONE (but not refuse) parental leave for up to 6 months if the business would be unduly disrupted — but cannot postpone where the leave is to begin on the date of the child's birth or adoption placement.

entitlement.amount: 18 weeks per child (not per year — total entitlement per child)
entitlement.paid: UNPAID — unless the employer's policy provides otherwise
entitlement.blocks: Must be taken in blocks of 1 WEEK (minimum), unless the child receives disability living allowance / personal independence payment, in which case leave can be taken in individual days
entitlement.maxPerYear: Maximum 4 weeks per child per year
entitlement.qualifyingPeriod: REMOVED from 6 April 2026 by ERA 2025 s.15 — becomes a day-one right. Previously: 1 year's continuous employment required.

employerPostponement.right: The employer may postpone parental leave for up to 6 months if the employer considers that the operation of the business would be UNDULY DISRUPTED (Reg.15(2)).
employerPostponement.cannotPostpone: The employer CANNOT postpone leave that is to begin on: (a) the date of the child's birth; or (b) the date of the child's adoption placement. These occasions are protected.
employerPostponement.procedure: The employer must give the employee notice of postponement in writing within 7 days of the request, stating the reason for postponement and the dates on which the employer agrees the leave can be taken.

protections.detriment: s.47C ERA 1996 — the employee has the right not to suffer detriment for taking or requesting parental leave.
protections.dismissal: s.99 ERA 1996 — dismissal for a reason connected to parental leave is automatically unfair. No qualifying period.
protections.complaint: s.80 ERA 1996 — the employee may present a complaint to the tribunal if the employer unreasonably postponed or prevented parental leave.

versions.amendedBy: ERA 1996 as enacted by ER Act 1999; ERA 2025 s.15 removes qualifying period from 6 April 2026. SI 2026/15 makes consequential amendments.

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 s.15 removes the 1-year qualifying period for parental leave — it becomes a DAY-ONE right from 6 April 2026. The entitlement amount (18 weeks), unpaid status, and other conditions are unchanged.
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: 6 April 2026 — IN FORCE in 7 days from session date.
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: MODERATE — an additional estimated 1.5 million parents gain immediate access to parental leave.

leadingCases.name: Rodway v South Central Trains Ltd
leadingCases.principle: Parental leave entitlements transfer with the employee when changing employers. If an employee has used 6 weeks of parental leave with Employer A, they have 12 weeks remaining when they join Employer B. The new employer inherits the balance.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that parental leave is a per-child entitlement, not a per-employer entitlement. The remaining balance transfers to the new employer.
leadingCases.significance: Confirms that parental leave is portable — it follows the child, not the employment relationship.

leadingCases.name: New Southern Railway v Rodway
leadingCases.principle: The employer's right to postpone parental leave under Reg.15 must be exercised within 7 days and must propose alternative dates. Failure to comply with the postponement procedure means the leave stands as requested.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal confirmed that the postponement procedure is mandatory. The employer must respond within 7 days with a written notice explaining the reason and proposing alternative dates.
leadingCases.significance: Confirms strict compliance with postponement procedure — failure to follow it means the employee can take the leave as requested.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Parental leave
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance on parental leave entitlements.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Parental leave
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance on parental leave rights.

commonErrors: Not knowing that parental leave becomes a DAY-ONE right from 6 April 2026 — ERA 2025 s.15 removes the 1-year qualifying period. From 6 April 2026, ALL employees with responsibility for a child can take parental leave from their first day. Contracts and handbooks referencing the 1-year qualifying period must be updated IMMEDIATELY

Confusing parental leave (unpaid, 18 weeks per child) with other family leave — parental leave is SEPARATE from maternity leave (52 weeks, partly paid), paternity leave (2 weeks, paid), shared parental leave (up to 50 weeks), and Carer's Leave (1 week). Each has its own entitlements and conditions

Refusing parental leave instead of postponing — the employer CANNOT refuse parental leave. The employer can only POSTPONE it for up to 6 months if the business would be unduly disrupted — and cannot postpone leave starting on the child's birth/placement date. A refusal is an unreasonable prevention under s.80

Not following the postponement procedure — the employer must give written notice of postponement within 7 DAYS of the request, stating the reason and proposing alternative dates. Failure to comply means the leave stands as requested (Rodway v South Central Trains [2005] EWCA)

Assuming parental leave resets with each employer — parental leave is a PER-CHILD entitlement (18 weeks total), NOT a per-employer entitlement. If an employee used 8 weeks with a previous employer, the new employer gets the remaining 10 weeks. The balance transfers (Rodway [2005])

Not recognising that dismissal for taking parental leave is automatically unfair — s.99 makes dismissal for a reason connected to parental leave automatically unfair with NO qualifying period. This applies from day one — and from 6 April 2026, the parental leave right itself is also day-one

Treating parental leave as annual leave or counting it against the employee — parental leave is a statutory right, not a discretionary benefit. The employer cannot require the employee to use annual leave instead, cannot count parental leave against their attendance record, and cannot use it as a factor in performance reviews or selection for redundancy

Not updating policies for 6 April 2026 — contracts, handbooks, and policies that reference the 1-year qualifying period for parental leave must be updated before 6 April 2026 (7 DAYS from this session). The ERA 2025 change is already in force for notice purposes (employees can give notice from 18 February 2026 for leave starting from 6 April 2026)
